title: Provenance: team, prize address, farewell, site wind-down

finding.phase: Provenance: team, prize address, farewell, site wind-down
finding.summary: The GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle remains genuinely unsolved past Phase 3.2, but the "provenance" claims published on the community site are a mix of solidly true and unverifiable. On-chain facts check out with one important correction: the prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe currently holds ~1.256 BTC (not 5 or 2.5), and it has SPENT outputs — so describing it as "unspent" is misleading. The funds were moved by the creators as part of a "halving" schedule (5 → 2.5 → 1.25 BTC), and the split-off address 17ucy1K9ZUAaoY6JVtM932W9jUp5LXfyHa does hold exactly 3.75 BTC, confirming the Naddiseo walkthrough. The team-handle list (JRK, d0d, Darky, Bloctite) and the 2026 farewell/wind-down message are sourced to a private GSMG Discord and cannot be corroborated in any public, indexed source — only "Jrk/jrk" is attested as the creator who posted official hints; the rest are unverified.
stepByStep: WHAT THIS TOPIC IS: 'Provenance' means the back-story and ownership trail of the puzzle - who made it, where the 5 BTC reward actually sits today, and what the creators said when (allegedly) shutting the project down. None of this is part of solving the cryptography; it is about verifying the surrounding facts. We check claims by looking at two things newcomers can also check: (a) the public Bitcoin ledger, and (b) independent write-ups by other solvers.

CONCEPT - WHAT A BITCOIN ADDRESS IS: Think of a Bitcoin address (the string starting with 1GSMG...) like a transparent glass piggy-bank with a number painted on it. Anyone in the world can SEE how much is inside and every coin that ever went in or came out, but only the person holding the matching secret 'private key' can take money out. The whole puzzle is a treasure hunt for that one private key.

CONCEPT - WHAT 'SPENT' vs 'UNSPENT' MEANS: Every coin sitting in an address is an 'unspent output' (UTXO). The moment it is moved elsewhere, that output becomes 'spent'. A block explorer reports two running totals: 'funded' (everything ever received) and 'spent' (everything ever sent out). Balance = funded minus spent. If 'spent' is greater than zero, the address has had money leave it.

STEP 1 - LOOK UP THE PRIZE ADDRESS: Open any free block explorer (mempool.space, blockchain.com, blockstream.info, or blockchair.com) and paste 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe. You will see: total received 875,988,008 satoshis (8.7599 BTC), total sent 750,353,498 satoshis (7.5035 BTC), and a current balance of 125,634,510 satoshis = 1.25634510 BTC, across 125 transactions. A satoshi is the penny of Bitcoin: 100,000,000 satoshis = 1 BTC.

STEP 2 - NOTICE THE 'SPENT' COUNT: The same explorer shows spent_txo_count = 6. That means six outputs have LEFT this address. So the often-repeated claim 'the prize address is unspent' is literally false: the address has moved 7.5 BTC out over its life. Why does that not mean someone solved the puzzle? Because the spends are the CREATORS deliberately reducing the prize on the 'halving' schedule, not a solver draining it - the remaining balance is exactly what the schedule predicts.

CONCEPT - 'HALVING': Bitcoin's own network roughly halves the rate of new coins every ~4 years (May 2020, April 2024). The puzzle creators mirrored this: every time Bitcoin halved, they cut the puzzle prize in half too, moving the other half away. So the prize went 5 BTC (2019) -> 2.5 BTC (after 11 May 2020) -> 1.25 BTC (after ~19 April 2024). The ~1.256 BTC you see today equals that 1.25 BTC plus a few thousand satoshis of 'dust' that random people have since sent in.

STEP 3 - CHECK THE SPLIT-OFF ADDRESS: Paste 17ucy1K9ZUAaoY6JVtM932W9jUp5LXfyHa into the same explorer. It shows total received 375,054,755 satoshis, total sent 0, balance = 3.75054755 BTC, and spent_txo_count = 0 (fully unspent). 3.75 = 2.5 + 1.25, i.e. the two halves that were peeled OFF the prize. This confirms the claim that a split-off address holds ~3.75 BTC and matches the Naddiseo walkthrough's statement exactly.

STEP 4 - CHECK THE DONATION ADDRESS: Paste 1JK27jtvE1wS4VG9k7Zpo8wBufMbYwy3r8. It exists, has received a handful of small donations (a few hundred thousand satoshis, with on-chain OP_RETURN notes like 'for ying yang thank you!'), and has never sent anything. Caveat: this is the donation/tip address listed in the OLDER puzzlehunt walkthrough README; the CURRENT Naddiseo README instead lists a newer bech32 address bc1qla7lmz3guzz3cezhfl72r0w546a4gyacpu530r. So 'the donation address' is not a single fixed value - it depends which write-up you read.

STEP 5 - CHECK WHO THE TEAM IS: Read the two independent community walkthroughs (github.com/puzzlehunt/gsmgio-5btc-puzzle and github.com/Naddiseo/gsmgio-5btc-puzzle). They refer to the makers only as 'us guys at GSMG' and name exactly ONE handle: 'Jrk' / 'jrk' - the person who posted official hints and announcements (e.g. on Discord and in the puzzle timeline). The other three names in the claim - d0d, Darky, Bloctite - do NOT appear in either walkthrough or in any search-indexed forum/Reddit/Bitcointalk thread. Treat only 'JRK' as attested; the rest are unverified.

STEP 6 - CHECK THE FAREWELL / WIND-DOWN STORY: Search the open web (Reddit r/codes, Bitcointalk, news sites) for any 2025/2026 GSMG 'farewell', 'shutting down', or 'community-maintained' announcement. Nothing public turns up. The Wayback Machine still has a snapshot of gsmg.io dated 10 January 2026 (so the URL had not vanished as of then), and the domain registration runs to ~3 July 2026. The specific farewell text - titled 'All Good Things Come To An End - The End of GSMG' / 'Our 9-Year Chaos Tour Is Officially Over' and signed 'JRK, d0d, Darky, Bloctite, and the remaining GSMG crew' - is attributed to the private GSMG Discord, 2026. Discord posts are not indexed by search engines and cannot be independently fetched, so this message can be neither confirmed nor disproved from public sources. Mark it UNVERIFIED, not confirmed.

STEP 7 - WHAT IS STILL OPEN (do not believe 'I solved it' posts): The cryptographic puzzle is SOLVED only through Phase 3.2. The endgame - two encoded blocks nicknamed 'dbbi' (target word 'yellowblueprimes') and 'faed' (target word 'yinyang'), which feed the final 'Cosmic Duality' decryption recipe sha256(yellowblueprimes . matrixsumlist . lastwordsbeforearchichoice . yinyang) - is UNSOLVED. Many confident 'solution' posts exist (e.g. GitHub issue #69), but none has ever produced a private key that actually moves the coins, which is the only real proof. The fact that the prize/split addresses still hold their coins is itself the proof nobody has truly solved it.

facts.claim: Prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe is UNSPENT
facts.value: REFUTED as literally stated: it has 6 spent outputs (spent_txo_count=6) and has sent out 7.50353498 BTC over its life. It is correct only in the narrow sense that no SOLVER has drained it; the creators moved funds via halving splits.
facts.note: The local repo README line 73 states the address is 'unspent'. On-chain chain_stats: funded 875,988,008 sat, spent 750,353,498 sat, spent_txo_count 6, tx_count 125.

facts.claim: Current balance of the prize address
facts.note: Equals the post-2024-halving 1.25 BTC plus a small amount of dust sent in by third parties. One WebFetch mis-divided this as 0.0126 BTC; the correct figure is 125,634,510/1e8 = 1.2563 BTC.

facts.claim: Split-off address 17ucy1K9ZUAaoY6JVtM932W9jUp5LXfyHa holds ~3.75 BTC after a halving split
facts.value: CONFIRMED: balance 3.75054755 BTC (375,054,755 sat), total sent 0, spent_txo_count 0 (fully unspent). Holds the 2.5+1.25 BTC peeled off the prize.
facts.note: The Naddiseo walkthrough explicitly states this address contains 3.75 BTC, created when the prize was split at the first halving; on-chain data matches exactly.

facts.claim: Donation/hint address 1JK27jtvE1wS4VG9k7Zpo8wBufMbYwy3r8
facts.value: CONFIRMED to exist and to have received small donations; it is the tip address listed in the OLDER puzzlehunt walkthrough. Balance ~0.0023 BTC, never spent. NOTE: the current Naddiseo walkthrough lists a different bech32 donation address (bc1qla7lmz3guzz3cezhfl72r0w546a4gyacpu530r).
facts.note: Has on-chain OP_RETURN note 'for ying yang thank you!'. 'The' donation address is not unique - it differs between the two community walkthroughs.

facts.claim: Halving schedule of the prize: 5 BTC -> 2.5 BTC (11 May 2020) -> 1.25 BTC (~19 Apr 2024)
facts.value: CONFIRMED by Naddiseo walkthrough and consistent with on-chain balances (1.256 BTC remaining in prize + 3.75 BTC in split-off). puzzlehunt walkthrough only documents the first step (to 2.5 BTC).
facts.note: Naddiseo: 'It happened for the first time on May 11, 2020, and a second time on Apr 19, 2024. the current value is 1.25 BTC.'

facts.claim: Team handles are JRK, d0d, Darky, Bloctite (and crew)
facts.value: PARTIALLY: only 'Jrk/jrk' is attested in independent walkthroughs as the creator who posts official hints. d0d, Darky, Bloctite appear ONLY in the local repo's own README/farewell and in no public, indexed source.
facts.note: Walkthroughs refer to the makers as 'us guys at GSMG' and name only 'Jrk'. The local index.html timeline cites 'jrk (Discord #4105)'. The three-name extension is uncorroborated.

facts.claim: Creators wound down gsmg.io and asked the puzzle to be community-maintained because the URL might vanish
facts.value: UNVERIFIED: no public source confirms a wind-down. A Wayback snapshot of gsmg.io exists dated 10 Jan 2026 (site still live), and the domain is registered until ~3 Jul 2026. The wind-down/community-maintenance request is sourced only to a private Discord and the local repo.
facts.note: The domain expiry (~2026-07-03) gives a plausible basis for 'the URL might vanish', but no public statement by the creators corroborates the wind-down claim.

facts.claim: A farewell message was posted around 2026
facts.value: UNVERIFIED: the local repo reproduces a farewell titled 'All Good Things Come To An End - The End of GSMG' / 'Our 9-Year Chaos Tour Is Officially Over', attributed to 'GSMG team, Discord, 2026', signed 'JRK, d0d, Darky, Bloctite, and the remaining GSMG crew'. No public/indexed source carries this text; Discord is not crawlable and web.archive.org was not fetchable here.
facts.note: Cannot be confirmed or refuted from public sources. The text exists in the local index.html (section with class 'farewell') but its only cited origin is the private GSMG Discord.

facts.claim: Overall puzzle solve status
facts.value: Solved through Phase 3.2; endgame (dbbi->yellowblueprimes, faed->yinyang, feeding Cosmic Duality) is OPEN. No public 'solution' has ever moved the coins; the unspent split-off and intact prize confirm it is unsolved.
facts.note: GitHub issue #69 ('SOLVED') and issue #84 ('unsolvable') are claims/opinions, not verified solutions - issue #69's own author admits both component addresses hold 0 BTC and no funds were moved.

discrepancies: The local repo README (line 73) calls the prize address 'unspent', but on-chain it has 6 spent outputs and has sent out 7.50353498 BTC. It is unspent only in the sense that no solver drained it; the creators moved the coins via halving splits. The wording should be 'the prize tranche is unclaimed by any solver', not 'the address is unspent'.

The two community walkthroughs disagree on the prize narrative: puzzlehunt says it was simply 'halved to 2.5 BTC' (and stops there); Naddiseo documents the full 5 -> 2.5 -> 1.25 schedule and names the 17ucy... split-off holding 3.75 BTC. The on-chain data supports the Naddiseo version.

The donation address is not a single fixed value: the older puzzlehunt README lists legacy 1JK27jtvE1wS4VG9k7Zpo8wBufMbYwy3r8 while the current Naddiseo README lists bech32 bc1qla7lmz3guzz3cezhfl72r0w546a4gyacpu530r. Both are real; the claim should specify which write-up it comes from.

Team handles d0d, Darky, Bloctite and the 2026 farewell text appear only in the local repo and an alleged private Discord post - no public, indexed source corroborates them. Only 'JRK/jrk' is independently attested. These should be presented as 'per the GSMG Discord (unverifiable publicly)', not as established fact.

A common trap: the news.bitcoin.com article 'The Solution to the World's Toughest Bitcoin Puzzle...' is about a DIFFERENT puzzle (1FLAMEN6 by coin_artist / Rob Myers, solved by 'Isaac'). It is NOT about GSMG and must not be used to claim the GSMG prize was claimed.
GitHub issue #69 is titled 'SOLVED' but is an unverified claim; its own author states the derived component addresses hold 0 BTC and that no funds were moved. It does not constitute a solution.
beginnerExplainers: Bitcoin address: like a see-through piggy-bank with a number on it - everyone can read the balance and full history, but only the holder of the secret private key can take coins out. The puzzle is a hunt for that key.
Satoshi: the smallest unit of Bitcoin, like a cent to a dollar. 100,000,000 satoshis = 1 BTC. So 125,634,510 satoshis = 1.2563 BTC.
Spent vs unspent output (UTXO): a coin sitting in an address is 'unspent'; once moved it becomes 'spent'. Explorers show 'spent_txo_count' - if it is above zero, money has left the address.
Block explorer: a free public website (mempool.space, blockchain.com, blockstream.info, blockchair.com) that lets anyone read the Bitcoin ledger - balances and every transaction - no account needed.

Bitcoin halving: roughly every four years Bitcoin cuts its new-coin issuance in half (2020, 2024). GSMG copied this idea by halving the puzzle prize on the same dates, peeling the other half into a separate address.

Private key: the secret number that controls an address. Knowing the address lets you watch the money; only knowing the private key lets you spend it - which is the only true proof anyone has 'solved' the puzzle.
Wayback Machine (web.archive.org): a free archive that periodically photographs web pages, so you can see what a site looked like on a past date even after it changes or disappears.

Why Discord can't be fact-checked: Discord chats live behind a login and are not crawled by search engines or the Wayback Machine, so a 'message posted on Discord' leaves no public, independently checkable trail.
OP_RETURN: a tiny note someone can attach to a Bitcoin transaction, permanently written into the ledger - here used to tag donations with short messages like 'for ying yang thank you!'.

verdict.phase: Provenance: team, prize address, farewell, site wind-down

checks.fact: Prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe is NOT unspent (spent_txo_count=6, ~7.5035 BTC sent out), so the local README 'unspent' wording is literally false.
checks.reasoning: Three independent explorers (mempool.space, blockstream.info, blockchain.info) all return identical figures: funded 875,988,008 sat, spent 750,353,498 sat, spent_txo_count 6, tx_count 125. The finding's refutation of 'unspent' is solidly correct; the nuance that no solver drained it (creators moved funds via halving) is consistent with the data.

checks.fact: Current balance of prize address is 1.25634510 BTC (125,634,510 satoshis).
checks.reasoning: blockchain.info reports final_balance 125,634,510 directly; the other two give funded minus spent = 125,634,510 = 1.2563451 BTC. Three independent sources agree exactly.

checks.fact: Split-off address 17ucy1K9ZUAaoY6JVtM932W9jUp5LXfyHa holds ~3.75 BTC, fully unspent (3.75 = 2.5 + 1.25).
checks.reasoning: mempool.space: funded 375,054,755 sat, spent 0, spent_txo_count 0. blockchain.info: total_received 375,054,755, total_sent 0, balance 375,054,755 = 3.75054755 BTC. Naddiseo README explicitly states a second address holding 3.75 BTC at exactly this string. On-chain and walkthrough agree.

checks.fact: Donation address 1JK27jtvE1wS4VG9k7Zpo8wBufMbYwy3r8 exists, has small donations, never spent; Naddiseo lists a different bech32 address bc1qla7lmz3guzz3cezhfl72r0w546a4gyacpu530r.
checks.reasoning: blockchain.info shows total_received 233,966 sat, total_sent 0, 4 tx (small never-spent balance, consistent with 'a few hundred thousand satoshis'). Naddiseo README confirms the bech32 donation string verbatim: 'please donate BTC at [bc1qla7lmz3guzz3cezhfl72r0w546a4gyacpu530r]'. The 'donation address is not a single fixed value' discrepancy is real.

checks.fact: Halving schedule 5 BTC -> 2.5 BTC (11 May 2020) -> 1.25 BTC (~19 Apr 2024).
checks.reasoning: Naddiseo README quote matches exactly: 'It happened for the first time on May 11, 2020, and a second time on Apr 19, 2024. the current value is 1.25 BTC.' Consistent with on-chain balances (1.256 in prize + 3.75 in split-off = 5.006, the ~0.006 being dust/donations).

checks.fact: Team handles are JRK, d0d, Darky, Bloctite (and crew).
checks.reasoning: Independent corroboration found ONLY for 'Jrk/jrk' (appears multiple times in Naddiseo README as the hint-poster; index.html cites 'jrk (Discord #4105)'). The names d0d, Darky, Bloctite appear ONLY in the local repo README line 82 and the local index.html farewell section. Two web searches surfaced no public/indexed source for them. Correctly flagged as unverified in the finding; I default to uncertain.

checks.fact: Creators wound down gsmg.io and asked for community maintenance because the URL might vanish (2026 farewell).
checks.reasoning: No public source confirms a wind-down. The Internet Archive availability API confirms a real gsmg.io snapshot at timestamp 20260110051955 (available:true) - i.e. the site existed as of 10 Jan 2026, which neither confirms nor refutes a later wind-down. The farewell text exists only in the local index.html (verified: 'farewell' CSS section present) and is attributed to a private Discord, which is not crawlable. Two targeted searches found no 'End of GSMG' announcement. Cannot confirm or refute; correctly UNVERIFIED.

checks.fact: A farewell message ('All Good Things Come To An End / Our 9-Year Chaos Tour Is Officially Over') was posted around 2026, signed JRK, d0d, Darky, Bloctite and crew.
checks.reasoning: Grep confirms the local index.html contains a 'farewell' section (CSS classes .farewell, .fwhead, .fwsig etc.) so the text exists locally. But its only cited origin is a private GSMG Discord, which search engines do not index and which the Wayback Machine cannot capture. WebFetch of web.archive.org is blocked in this environment. No public/indexed source carries this text. Neither confirmable nor refutable - correctly UNVERIFIED.

checks.fact: Puzzle is solved only through Phase 3.2; endgame is open; no public 'solution' has moved the coins.
checks.reasoning: GitHub issue #69 (titled 'SOLVED') author admits verbatim 'Both component addresses hold 0 BTC' and provides no blockchain verification that any funds moved - it is a theoretical claim, not a solution. The intact prize (1.256 BTC) and untouched split-off (3.75 BTC, 0 spent) are themselves proof no one has truly solved it. Multiple independent walkthroughs treat it as unsolved past 3.2.

checks.fact: Red flag: news.bitcoin.com 'Solution to the World's Toughest Bitcoin Puzzle' is a DIFFERENT puzzle (1FLAMEN6 by coin_artist/Rob Myers, solved by 'Isaac'), not GSMG.
checks.reasoning: Search confirms the article concerns the 1FLAMEN6 'Legend of Satoshi Nakamoto' painting by coin_artist with Rob Myers, solved by a programmer pseudonymed 'Isaac'. It has nothing to do with GSMG and must not be used to claim the GSMG prize was claimed. The finding's caution is correct.

redFlags: Team handles d0d, Darky, Bloctite and the entire 2026 farewell text are self-sourced: they appear only in the local repo (README line 82, index.html 'farewell' section) and an alleged private Discord. No public, indexed source corroborates them. The finding itself correctly marks these unverified - do NOT let the otherwise-solid on-chain facts lend them false credibility.

The local README line 73 and CLAUDE.md line 48 both assert the prize address is 'unspent', which is literally false on-chain (6 spent outputs, 7.5 BTC sent). This is an internal inconsistency in the repo that the finding flags but the repo still ships.

Discord-sourced claims are structurally unfalsifiable here: web.archive.org is not fetchable in this environment, and Discord is not search-indexed. Any 'confirmation' of the farewell/wind-down would be over-confident; uncertain is the correct ceiling.

GitHub issue #69 is titled 'SOLVED' but its own author admits both component addresses hold 0 BTC and shows no moved funds - a classic over-confident fabricated 'solution' that must not be treated as a real solve.

title: Phase 0 — genesis image, the 14x14 matrix, theseedisplanted

finding.phase: Phase 0 — Genesis image, the 14x14 binary matrix, and the decoded URL gsmg.io/theseedisplanted
finding.summary: Phase 0 is the puzzle's entry point: a 14x14 grid of coloured squares (a custom "image", not a standard QR code) that encodes binary. Black/blue squares = 1, yellow/white squares = 0. Reading the cells in a counter-clockwise spiral starting at the upper-left corner gives 196 bits = 24.5 bytes; grouping into 8-bit bytes and converting to ASCII spells "gsmg.io/theseedisplanted" (followed by a null padding bit). This URL is the door to the rest of the puzzle. The grid contains exactly 101 "1" cells; its row sums (610876654997879) and column sums (8108108736759668) are a SEPARATE, later-used artifact that feeds the token "matrixsumlist" used in the endgame — they are not needed to get the URL. I independently reproduced both the spiral decode (→ the exact URL) and both sum strings from the matrix, so the core mechanics are fully confirmed. The puzzle remains unsolved; the prize address still holds its dominant (now ~1.25 BTC) prize UTXO.
stepByStep: WHAT YOU ARE LOOKING AT: The puzzle starts with a single picture made of small coloured squares arranged in a perfect 14-by-14 grid (14 across, 14 down = 196 squares total). It is NOT a normal QR code you can scan with a phone; it is a hand-made grid that you must decode by hand. Each square is one of two 'kinds': a dark one (described as black or blue) or a light one (described as yellow or white).

CONCEPT — BINARY (bits): Computers store everything as 1s and 0s, called 'bits'. A single square that can be one of two colours is a perfect way to store one bit. The puzzle's rule (stated by the creators and repeated in every walkthrough) is: a black/blue square means the bit 1, and a yellow/white square means the bit 0. So the whole picture is really a wall of 196 ones and zeros.

WRITE DOWN THE GRID AS NUMBERS: Go square by square and replace each colour with its bit. You get this exact 14x14 grid of 0s and 1s (row by row, top to bottom): 00110100101100 / 11110011101011 / 11011101001001 / 01101000011101 / 01100011000110 / 10011000100011 / 10011100010000 / 11100000001000 / 00011101111101 / 11111100110001 / 11010000011011 / 11110010101100 / 01011101000110 / 01101101101011. (Both independent community walkthroughs list this identical grid.)

CONCEPT — READING ORDER MATTERS: 196 bits can be read in many orders (left-to-right rows, top-to-bottom columns, etc.). The puzzle tells you the correct order is a SPIRAL: 'Start from the upper-left square and go counter-clockwise in a spiral.' Imagine peeling an onion from the outside in, but instead of around-and-around clockwise, you go anti-clockwise.

TRACE THE SPIRAL (concretely): Starting at the top-left square, go DOWN the entire left-hand column. When you hit the bottom-left corner, turn and go RIGHT along the whole bottom row. At the bottom-right corner, turn and go UP the whole right-hand column. At the top-right, go LEFT along the top row (stopping one square short of where you began, because that square is already used). You have now traced the outer ring counter-clockwise. Repeat the same down-right-up-left pattern on the next ring inside, then the next, until every one of the 196 squares is used exactly once. Writing down each bit as you visit it gives one long string of 196 bits.

CONCEPT — BYTES AND ASCII: Text is stored as numbers using a standard table called ASCII. Each character is one 'byte' = 8 bits. For example the 8 bits 01100111 read as a binary number equal 103, and ASCII says 103 = the letter 'g'. So you can turn bits back into letters by chopping the bit-string into groups of 8 and looking each group up.

CHOP AND CONVERT: Take the 196-bit spiral string and split it into 8-bit groups: 01100111, 01110011, 01101101, 01100111, ... Convert each to its ASCII letter: 01100111=g, 01110011=s, 01101101=m, 01100111=g, then '.', 'i', 'o', '/', then 't,h,e,s,e,e,d,i,s,p,l,a,n,t,e,d'. The final 4 leftover bits are zero padding (a null character) and are ignored.

READ THE RESULT: The letters spell out the web address: gsmg.io/theseedisplanted. (196 bits / 8 = 24 full characters + a 4-bit zero remainder. The 24 characters are exactly 'gsmg.io/theseedisplanted'.)

GO THERE: Typing https://gsmg.io/theseedisplanted into a browser (back when the site was live) loaded the next stage — 'The Seed Is Planted' — which begins Phase 1 (the hidden password form, answer 'theflowerblossomsthroughwhatseemstobeaconcretesurface'). The genesis matrix's only job is to hand you this URL.

ABOUT THE BLUE AND YELLOW (the highlighted cells): Some squares are specifically coloured BLUE or YELLOW rather than plain black/white. For getting the URL this makes NO difference — blue counts as 1 like black, yellow as 0 like white, so the decode is identical. Their special colouring is a SIGNPOST to a different, later use of the grid: the creators' official 2020-01-14 'Roses are Red' hint says 'Yellow has a number and so does Blue', pointing solvers to treat the grid as NUMBERS (its sums), not just letters.

THE ROW AND COLUMN SUMS (a separate artifact): Count how many 1s are in each row: 6,10,8,7,6,6,5,4,9,9,7,8,7,9. Stuck together as digits this reads 610876654997879. Count the 1s in each column: 8,10,8,10,8,7,3,6,7,5,9,6,6,8 → 8108108736759668. Both totals add up to 101 (a sanity check that the same 101 cells are counted both ways). These two number-strings later combine into the token 'matrixsumlist', one of several passphrases used much deeper in the puzzle.

WHY IT ALL WORKS (the big picture): The matrix is a self-contained, software-free message. Colour→bit turns a picture into data; the spiral is the agreed reading order; ASCII turns data back into human text; the text is a URL. Nothing here needs a computer beyond pen, paper, and an ASCII table — which is exactly the point of a 'genesis' clue: anyone, anywhere can start.

facts.claim: The starting artifact is a 14x14 binary matrix of coloured squares (not a scannable standard QR code).
facts.note: Both independent walkthroughs (puzzlehunt, Naddiseo) describe a '14x14 binary matrix'. It is sometimes loosely called a 'QR code' in summaries, but it is a custom hand-decoded grid, not an ISO QR code. The 196 cells = 24.5 bytes.

facts.claim: Colour-to-bit mapping: black/blue squares = bit 1, yellow/white squares = bit 0.
facts.note: Stated verbatim in puzzlehunt README ('Squares are bits, black/blue - 1 and yellow/white - 0') and in Naddiseo's notebook (b='1', y='0').

facts.claim: The reading order is a counter-clockwise spiral starting at the upper-left square.
facts.note: puzzlehunt: 'Start from upper left square and go counterclockwise in a spiral.' Naddiseo's notebook implements exactly this (left/bottom/right/top peeling). I re-implemented the spiral independently and it reproduces the URL, confirming the path.

facts.claim: Decoding the spiral bits as 8-bit ASCII yields the URL gsmg.io/theseedisplanted.
facts.note: Both walkthroughs give this exact string. I independently ran a counter-clockwise spiral + 8-bit ASCII decode on the agreed matrix and got 'gsmg.io/theseedisplanted' followed by one null padding byte. 01100111=103='g' etc. verified.

facts.claim: The matrix has highlighted blue and yellow cells (distinct from plain black/white).
facts.value: blue and yellow accent cells exist
facts.note: Naddiseo's notebook encodes specific cells as 'b' (blue) and 'y' (yellow), distinct from plain 0/1. For the URL they decode identically (b=1, y=0). beginnerExplainers: Bit: the smallest piece of computer information — just a 1 or a 0. A square that can be one of two colours stores exactly one bit, like a light switch that's either on or off.
Binary: counting with only 1s and 0s instead of the usual ten digits. The whole 14x14 picture is really 196 of these on/off switches.
Byte: a group of 8 bits. Computers handle text one byte at a time, and each byte stands for one character.
ASCII: a fixed lookup table that says which number means which character, e.g. 103 = 'g'. It lets you turn the on/off pattern back into readable letters.

Spiral reading order: the agreed path for visiting the squares — start top-left and wind inward going counter-clockwise (down, then right, then up, then left, ring by ring), like peeling an onion the anti-clockwise way.
Row/column sum: just counting how many '1' squares sit in each line. Sticking those counts together makes a long number the puzzle reuses later (named 'matrixsumlist').

Bitcoin address: a public account number (here 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe) that holds the prize; anyone can see its balance, but only the secret key — the puzzle's final answer — can spend it.

Unspent / UTXO: a chunk of bitcoin sitting in an address that has never been moved out. The big ~1.25 BTC chunk here is still unspent, which is how we know nobody has solved the puzzle and taken the prize.
discrepancies: '5 BTC unspent' is an oversimplification. On-chain the prize address shows MANY transactions and a large spent total (~7.5 BTC moved historically) plus ~150 tiny 546-satoshi dust UTXOs sent by spectators. This is NOT a solver claiming the prize — it reflects the creators reducing/re-funding the prize at each halving (5 -> 2.5 -> ~1.25 BTC) and onlooker dust spam. The single dominant prize UTXO (~1.25 BTC) is unspent, so the puzzle is genuinely unsolved. Beware reading raw explorer 'spent' figures as evidence of a solution.
Calling the genesis artifact a 'QR code' (as some summaries do) is loose. It is a bespoke 14x14 colour grid decoded by a custom spiral rule, not a standard scannable QR code.

The row-sum/column-sum strings are real and reproduce exactly, but they are NOT part of obtaining the genesis URL. The puzzlehunt README explicitly notes its Phase-0 section contains 'no mention of row sums or column sums' — those sums belong to the later 'matrixsumlist' artifact. Presenting them as a Phase 0 decoding step would be misleading.
Several public 'SOLVED' claims (e.g. puzzlehunt issues #56, #69) exist but none has produced a spendable key; the prize UTXO remains unmoved. Treat such claims as unverified.
The endgame tokens 'yellowblueprimes' (from block dbbi), 'yinyang' (from block faed), and the final Cosmic Duality decryption remain genuinely OPEN — do not treat any reconstructed key as confirmed.

finding.summary: "The Warning" is the first interactive web stage of the GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle. After the genesis image (a 14x14 colored grid) is decoded to the URL gsmg.io/theseedisplanted, that page hides a POST form (visible in browser dev tools) plus images that rearrange into "The Warning" by the band Logic. The required passphrase, theflowerblossomsthroughwhatseemstobeaconcretesurface, is a lyric line from that song (the line right after the word "Phase two"), lowercased with spaces removed. Submitting it redirects the solver to gsmg.io/choiceisanillusioncreatedbetweenthosewithpowerandthosewithoutaveryspecialdessertiwroteitmyself, a string stitched together from the Merovingian's monologue in The Matrix Reloaded; that next page begins the AES-encrypted Phase 2 (whose key derives from the word "causality"). Both verified phrases are confirmed by independent community walkthroughs; this stage is fully SOLVED and is purely a navigation/lookup step that reveals no bitcoin private key.
stepByStep: WHAT THE WHOLE PUZZLE IS: GSMG.io published a picture and a promise: anyone who can solve the chain of riddles hidden inside it can unlock a Bitcoin wallet holding a prize (originally 5 BTC). 'Bitcoin' is internet money; a 'wallet' is protected by a secret number called a 'private key'. Whoever first reconstructs that key can take the coins. The puzzle is split into stages; the one we cover here is the first web stage, nicknamed 'The Warning'.

STARTING POINT (the step BEFORE The Warning): The puzzle begins with one square image made of a 14x14 grid of colored tiles. The trick: treat colored tiles as the binary digits 1 and 0 (the puzzle's convention: black/blue tile = 1, yellow/white tile = 0). 'Binary' just means writing information using only two symbols, the way light switches are on/off. Reading the tiles by spiraling counter-clockwise and grouping them 8-at-a-time (8 bits = 1 'byte' = 1 text character, via the standard ASCII table that maps numbers to letters) spells out a web address: gsmg.io/theseedisplanted. (Some write-ups call this 'Phase 0' and call the page below 'Phase 1'; the community site you are checking calls the matrix 'Phase 1' and this page 'The Warning'. The naming differs but the steps are identical.)

OPEN THE PAGE: Type gsmg.io/theseedisplanted into a web browser. Historically this loaded a simple GSMG page. The visible page looks like a near-dead end, but the answer is hidden in two places: (a) inside the page's source code, and (b) in the images shown on it.

FIND THE HIDDEN FORM: Press F12 in Chrome (or right-click and choose 'Inspect') to open 'developer tools' — a panel that shows the raw HTML the page is built from, including parts not drawn on screen. There you find a hidden HTML form that does an HTTP 'POST' (a POST is the browser's way of submitting a secret answer to the server, instead of just requesting a page). The community walkthroughs note it posts to gsmg.io/phase1verification. This form is the locked door; it wants a password.

IDENTIFY THE THEME — 'The Warning' by Logic: The images on the page are scrambled. When you mentally rearrange the picture fragments, they spell the band and the song: 'war' + 'ning' = WARNING, and 'LO' + (a crypto/logic graphic) = LOGIC. So the clue is the song 'The Warning' by the artist Logic. This is the 'why': the page is literally named/themed around that track, telling you to go read its words.

GET THE PASSWORD FROM THE LYRICS: 'The Warning' contains the spoken/written line: 'The flower blossoms through what seems to be a concrete surface, ie. greed, racism, insanity, physical and social handicaps.' In the song this line comes right after the words 'Phase two' — a deliberate signpost the puzzle authors picked because their own puzzle has phases. You take just the sentence 'The flower blossoms through what seems to be a concrete surface', make every letter lowercase, and delete all spaces and punctuation. The result is the password: theflowerblossomsthroughwhatseemstobeaconcretesurface.

SUBMIT IT: Put that exact string into the hidden form and submit (POST) it. 'Lowercase, no spaces' is a common puzzle convention because a server checks the answer by an exact character-for-character match, so capitalization or a stray space would fail.

READ THE REDIRECT: A correct submission sends the browser to a new, very long URL: gsmg.io/choiceisanillusioncreatedbetweenthosewithpowerandthosewithoutaveryspecialdessertiwroteitmyself . That long word-salad is two quotations glued together from the SAME scene in the film The Matrix Reloaded, spoken by the character the Merovingian: 'Choice is an illusion, created between those with power, and those without.' and, moments later about the dessert he sends, 'a very special dessert. I wrote it myself.' The authors stripped spaces and joined them, the same lowercase-no-spaces style as before.

WHY THE MATRIX QUOTE MATTERS: It is not just decoration — it sets up the NEXT stage. The Merovingian's whole speech is about 'causality' (cause and effect). On the new page the solver is led to the password 'causality', whose SHA-256 hash (a hash is a one-way fingerprint of text: same input always gives the same fixed-length code, but you cannot run it backwards) becomes the key that decrypts the AES-256-CBC encrypted Phase 2 text. SHA256('causality') = eb3efb5151e6255994711fe8f2264427ceeebf88109e1d7fad5b0a8b6d07e5bf. (That decryption is the start of Phase 2, beyond this topic.)

WHAT YOU HAVE / HAVE NOT WON: Completing 'The Warning' gives you the next URL only — it reveals NO bitcoin private key and unlocks NO money by itself. It is a pure navigation-and-lookup gate. The real prize is locked at the very end of the puzzle (the still-unsolved 'Cosmic Duality' stage). The prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe is still unspent, so nobody has finished the whole puzzle.

Live blockchain data (blockchain.info / mempool.space) shows ~1.256 BTC, consistent with two halvings, confirming no solver has emptied it. beginnerExplainers: Binary: writing data with only two symbols, 0 and 1 — like a row of light switches each either off (0) or on (1). The puzzle's grid uses tile colors as those 0s and 1s.
Bit and byte: a 'bit' is one 0-or-1; a 'byte' is 8 bits grouped together. Eight bits make exactly one text character in the standard ASCII code table, which is why the grid is read 8 tiles at a time.
ASCII: an agreed-upon lookup table that pairs each number with a letter or symbol (e.g. 71 = 'G'), so a string of bytes becomes readable text like a URL.

HTML and a hidden form: a web page is built from HTML code; some of it is not drawn on screen. A 'form' is the part that lets you type an answer and send it back. Pressing F12 opens the browser's developer tools to read that hidden HTML.
HTTP POST: the method a browser uses to send your typed answer secretly to the server for checking, instead of just asking for a page to view.

Hash (SHA-256): a one-way 'fingerprint' machine for text — the same words always produce the same fixed-length code, but you cannot reverse the code back into the words. Here it turns the word 'causality' into the secret key for the next stage's encryption.
Encryption / AES-256-CBC: scrambling a message with a key so only someone with the key can unscramble (decrypt) it. AES is a widely used scrambling method; the puzzle hides Phase 2's text this way.

Bitcoin private key vs address: the 'address' is like a public mailbox anyone can see and send coins to; the 'private key' is the secret that lets you spend what is inside. The whole puzzle is a race to reconstruct that private key — but 'The Warning' alone reveals none of it.

Lowercase-no-spaces convention: puzzle answers are often normalized (all small letters, no spaces) because the server checks them by an exact character match, so any capital letter or stray space would be rejected.
discrepancies: Phase numbering differs between sources: Naddiseo labels the genesis binary matrix 'Phase 0' and the theseedisplanted hidden-form step 'Phase 1'; the puzzlehunt walkthrough and the community site label the matrix 'Phase 1' and call this hidden-form step 'The Warning'. Same steps, different names — not a contradiction.

Prize amount is stated inconsistently across snapshots: README/DeepWiki text says '2.5 BTC' (reflecting only the May 2020 halving), one search summary says '1.5 BTC', while live chain data shows ~1.256 BTC (consistent with TWO halvings, 2020 and 2024). The ~1.25 BTC figure is the most current and correct; the '2.5 BTC' note is simply stale, and '1.5 BTC' appears to be an approximation/error.

The address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe shows 125 transactions and ongoing small activity into 2026, which superficially looks 'spent'. This is because it is the puzzle's branded prize/receiving address, not a single frozen UTXO; the key point — that no solver has swept the prize and the puzzle remains unsolved — still holds. The separate donation address is 1JK27jtvE1wS4VG9k7Zpo8wBufMbYwy3r8.

finding.summary: Phase 2 of the GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle is a "Mr. Robot"-themed layer. Its first encrypted page is unlocked with the AES passphrase sha256("causality"). That page contains a riddle (the "# X 2 S H 4 Y 0 Q B 15 #" line plus Q/B/H/S clues and a Mr. Robot episode tag) whose answers build a single seven-part password: causality + Safenet + Luna + HSM + 11110 + the reversed Bitcoin genesis-block coinbase message (as a 0x-prefixed hex string) + a chess FEN. SHA-256 of that whole concatenated string is the AES passphrase for the next phase. I independently reproduced every published hash, the salt, the genesis-message reversal, and verified the chain end-to-end by actually decrypting the Phase 2 and Phase 3 blobs; all crypto values check out exactly. The one imprecise claim is the README's statement that the prize address is "unspent": the address has 125 transactions and prior spends (prize-halving moves by the creators), though the puzzle itself remains genuinely unsolved as of 2026.
stepByStep: WHAT THIS PHASE IS. By Phase 2 you have already passed the entrance (Phase 1, the 'theseedisplanted' page). Phase 2 is a locked text file (a 'ciphertext') that you must decrypt twice over: once to read its riddle, and the riddle's answer is then turned into the key that unlocks the NEXT phase. The whole puzzle works like a chain of locked boxes where the answer inside each box is reshaped into the key for the next box.

CONCEPT — WHAT 'ENCRYPTED' MEANS HERE. The file ciphertexts/phase2.txt looks like random gibberish (it begins 'U2FsdGVkX18...'). That is base64 text wrapping AES-256-CBC encrypted data produced by the OpenSSL tool. 'AES' is a standard lock; to open it you need the exact password the creator used. Decode the base64 and the raw bytes literally start with the ASCII word 'Salted__' followed by 8 'salt' bytes — this is OpenSSL's standard file header.

CONCEPT — WHAT A HASH IS, AND WHY IT'S USED AS THE KEY. A hash (SHA-256) is a one-way meat-grinder: feed it any text and it spits out a fixed 64-hex-character fingerprint; you cannot run it backwards. GSMG uses the SHA-256 fingerprint of the puzzle answer as the AES password. So 'find the answer' and 'find the key' are the same task.

STEP 1 — UNLOCK THE PHASE 2 PAGE WITH 'causality'. The first password is the single word causality (a Matrix Reloaded reference — the Merovingian's speech about 'cause and effect / causality'). You do NOT type 'causality' straight into the lock; you type its SHA-256 fingerprint. sha256('causality') = eb3efb5151e6255994711fe8f2264427ceeebf88109e1d7fad5b0a8b6d07e5bf. I recomputed this and it matches exactly.

STEP 1 (how to actually run it). With OpenSSL installed, run: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -a -md sha256 -in ciphertexts/phase2.txt -pass pass:eb3efb5151e6255994711fe8f2264427ceeebf88109e1d7fad5b0a8b6d07e5bf  — the '-md sha256' part is REQUIRED (older OpenSSL defaults to MD5 and would fail). The in-browser console on the community site does the identical math for you.

STEP 2 — READ THE RIDDLE THAT APPEARS. Decryption reveals a Mr. Robot-flavoured text. Verbatim it talks about 'the keymakers ... security by hiding ... in plain sight', tags itself '{eps3.4_[in one of the valleys of Phillip]runtime-error.r00...}' (a real Mr. Robot Season 3 episode-title style), and then gives a puzzle line: '# X 2 S H 4 Y 0 Q B 15 #' followed by four sub-clues: 'Q -> extend the name of a hackers swordless fish, the I and W are below.', 'B -> ((BV80605001911AP)-(sqrt(-1)))^2', 'H -> (Answer to only this puzzle but nothing else) * -1', 'S -> cha + (vagh * jav)'. It ends 'Ok kid, on the highway, let put it in the worst gear.' These clues, plus outside research, produce the seven password parts.

CONCEPT — WHY 'IN PLAIN SIGHT / KEYMAKERS' POINTS AT HSMs. The riddle is about people who guard digital keys ('keymakers'). In the real world, keys are guarded by a Hardware Security Module (HSM). The best-known HSM product line is Thales/Gemalto's 'SafeNet Luna' HSM. That single real-world product name supplies THREE of the seven parts: Safenet, Luna, and HSM. (The irony the text points at: a device meant to hide keys is itself named openly in the clue.)

STEP 3 — ASSEMBLE THE SEVEN PARTS. Part 1 = causality (the word you already used). Part 2 = Safenet. Part 3 = Luna. Part 4 = HSM. Part 5 = 11110 (read on). Part 6 = the 0x-hex genesis string (read on). Part 7 = the chess FEN (read on).

STEP 4 — PART 5, '11110' AND EXECUTIVE ORDER 11110. The community attributes 11110 to U.S. Executive Order 11110 (signed by JFK in 1963, touching Federal-Reserve/silver-certificate authority) — thematically fitting a money/banking puzzle. Honesty note: both independent walkthroughs say the EXACT logic for landing on 11110 from the on-page clues was partly brute-forced/never fully published, so treat 'why 11110' as plausible-but-not-airtight, even though the value 11110 itself is confirmed because it is what makes the final hash come out right.

STEP 5 — PART 6, THE BITCOIN GENESIS MESSAGE IN REVERSED HEX. Satoshi Nakamoto embedded a headline in Bitcoin's very first block (the 'genesis block' coinbase): 'The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks'. Part 6 is the hex 0x736B6E61...656854. Decoding that hex to text gives 'sknab rof tuoliab dnoces fo knirb no rollecnahC 9002/naJ/30 semiT ehT' — i.e. the genesis sentence spelled BACKWARDS. I decoded the hex and reversed it; it reproduces the genesis headline character-for-character. So Part 6 = that genesis message, stored as its reversed-hex form, kept WITH the leading '0x'.

STEP 6 — PART 7, THE CHESS FEN. A FEN is a one-line text snapshot of a chessboard. The clue presents a position where (in the walkthrough's words) 'a Buddhist is forced to move' — a Buddhist is a pacifist, so the required move is the one that does NOT deliver checkmate. The answer position is the FEN B5KR/1r5B/2R5/2b1p1p1/2P1k1P1/1p2P2p/1P2P2P/3N1N2 b - - 0 1 (note the 'b' meaning it is now Black's turn, after White's non-mating move). The whole FEN string is Part 7.

STEP 7 — CONCATENATE EXACTLY (this is the subtle part). Glue the seven parts with NO separators: causality + Safenet + Luna + HSM + 11110 + 0x736B6E616220726F662074756F6C69616220646E6F63657320666F206B6E697262206E6F20726F6C6C65636E61684320393030322F6E614A2F33302073656D695420656854 + B5KR/1r5B/2R5/2b1p1p1/2P1k1P1/1p2P2p/1P2P2P/3N1N2 b - - 0 1. Two details matter: keep the literal '0x' in front of the hex, and include the FULL FEN (not just 'B5KR'). I tested all four combinations; ONLY '0x-prefix + full FEN' reproduces the published next-phase hash.

STEP 8 — HASH THE WHOLE THING TO GET THE NEXT KEY. SHA-256 of that exact concatenated string = 1a57c572caf3cf722e41f5f9cf99ffacff06728a43032dd44c481c77d2ec30d5. I computed this myself and it matches the published value. This 64-hex string is the AES passphrase for Phase 3.

STEP 9 — PROVE THE CHAIN WORKS. Using 1a57c572... as the password to decrypt ciphertexts/phase3.txt yields readable text that continues the story ('What if the merovingian is wrong... The thinker's name behind all of that would grant you access...') and even hands you the Phase 3.2 ciphertext. A wrong key would produce garbage or a padding error, so a clean, on-theme decrypt is strong proof the Phase 2 password is correct. This is the end-to-end confirmation that the seven parts are right.

CONCEPT — WHAT'S SOLVED VS OPEN. Phase 2 is fully SOLVED and reproducible (everything above checks out). The overall puzzle is solved through Phase 3.2; the genuinely OPEN frontier is much later (the 'Cosmic Duality' box needing yellowblueprimes from block 'dbbi' and yinyang from block 'faed'). Do not let anyone tell you Phase 2 is unsolved, and do not believe anyone who claims the FINAL prize is cracked — the prize is still unclaimed.

beginnerExplainers: Hash (SHA-256): a one-way fingerprinting machine — any text in, a fixed 64-character code out, and you can't run it backwards. Like blending fruit into a smoothie: you can't un-blend it back into the original fruit.
AES-256-CBC encryption: a strong digital padlock; the same secret password both locks and unlocks the data. Wrong password = jammed lock (you get garbage or an error).

OpenSSL 'Salted__' header + salt: the locked file literally begins with the word 'Salted__' followed by 8 random 'salt' bytes (here 06286612d43ed7ed). Salt is like adding a pinch of randomness to the recipe so the same password produces different-looking locked files — it's stored in plain view inside the file, not secret.

EVP_BytesToKey / key derivation: OpenSSL stretches your password+salt through repeated SHA-256 to manufacture the actual 32-byte AES key and 16-byte IV. You don't see this; it's why '-md sha256' must be specified or the stretching uses the wrong recipe.
Base64: a way to write raw binary as safe printable letters/numbers (the 'U2FsdGVkX18...' text). It's an envelope, not a lock — decoding it just gets you back the raw encrypted bytes.
Hex (hexadecimal): a way to write bytes as pairs of 0-9/a-f characters. '0x' is just a label meaning 'what follows is hex'. Two hex characters = one byte = one text character here.

Bitcoin genesis block coinbase message: the very first Bitcoin block (Jan 2009) had a newspaper headline 'The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks' baked into it by Satoshi as a timestamp/protest. The puzzle hides this same sentence in reversed-hex form.

Chess FEN: a single line of text that photographs an entire chessboard position — which pieces sit on which squares, and whose turn it is (the lone 'b' = Black to move). 'Buddhist forced to move' is a riddle hint meaning 'play the peaceful move that does NOT checkmate'.

HSM / SafeNet Luna: a Hardware Security Module is a tamper-proof gadget whose only job is to guard secret cryptographic keys. 'SafeNet Luna' is a famous real HSM product — the puzzle's joke is that a key-hiding device is named openly 'in plain sight', supplying three password parts at once.

Bitcoin address 'spent vs unspent': an address is a public account number; explorers show every payment in and out. 'Unspent' means coins are still sitting there untouched. This puzzle's prize address actually HAS moved coins (the creators halve the prize over time), so 'unspent' is the wrong word even though no solver has claimed the prize.
discrepancies: README claim 'the prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe is unspent' is REFUTED by blockchain explorers: the address has 125 transactions and ~7.5 BTC of outgoing spends. The correct statement is 'the puzzle prize is unclaimed/unsolved' — the spends are the creators' scheduled prize-halving moves, not a solve. finding.summary: Phase 3 ("Free Will") is a SOLVED stage of the GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle. A previously decrypted blob (phase3.txt, OpenSSL salt 9fbc451d13d071f4) contains three short riddles whose answers are jacquefresco, giveitjustonesecond, and heisenbergsuncertaintyprinciple. Joined together with no spaces, in lowercase, they form the string jacquefrescogiveitjustonesecondheisenbergsuncertaintyprinciple, whose SHA-256 is 250f37726d6862939f723edc4f993fde9d33c6004aab4f2203d9ee489d61ce4c. That hash is the AES-256-CBC password that decrypts the next blob (phase3.2 / phase32.txt), revealing the "Architect" speech from The Matrix Reloaded. I verified every value directly: I recomputed the SHA-256, extracted the salt from the raw ciphertext, and decrypted both blobs with OpenSSL.
stepByStep: WHAT THIS PHASE IS: By this point in the puzzle, an earlier step has already handed you an encrypted text file (the community calls it phase3.txt). 'Encrypted' means the text was scrambled with a secret password so that only someone with the password can read it. Decrypting it reveals three little riddles. Your job in Phase 3 is to solve those three riddles, glue the answers together, turn that into a new password, and use it to unlock the NEXT file.

BACKGROUND CONCEPT 1 - What a 'hash' (SHA-256) is: A hash is a one-way fingerprinting machine. You feed in any text and it spits out a fixed-length string of 64 hex characters (digits 0-9 and letters a-f). The same input ALWAYS gives the same output, but you cannot run it backwards to recover the input. The puzzle uses SHA-256 to turn human-readable answers into the long random-looking password an encryption program expects.

BACKGROUND CONCEPT 2 - What AES / OpenSSL encryption is: AES-256-CBC is a standard recipe for scrambling data with a password. OpenSSL is a free program that does it. When OpenSSL encrypts with a password it prepends a marker: the bytes 'Salted__' followed by an 8-byte random 'salt'. The salt is just public seasoning mixed with your password so the same password produces different-looking output each time - it is NOT secret and is stored right at the front of the file. In base64 (a way of writing binary as plain letters) 'Salted__' always shows up as the visible prefix 'U2FsdGVkX1'. Every GSMG blob starts with that, which is how you know it is OpenSSL AES.

STEP 1 - Read the three riddles. Decrypting phase3.txt (with the password produced by the PREVIOUS phase) gives plain text containing three numbered hints. Riddle 1 reads: 'What if the merovingian is wrong... if so, the ...... is ours. The thinker's 1name behind all of that would grant you access to the next step (of humanity).' The blanked-out word (the ......) is 'free will' - which is why this phase is nicknamed Free Will.

STEP 2 - Solve Riddle 1 (the thinker). 'The thinker' who built a whole philosophy around humanity directing its own future / free will is Jacque Fresco, founder of The Venus Project. His famous quote: 'The future is fluid. Each act, each decision, and each development creates new possibilities and eliminates others. The future is ours to direct.' The instruction says use 'his name', so the answer is his name with no spaces, lowercase: jacquefresco.

STEP 3 - Solve Riddle 2 (Alice in Wonderland). The text reads: 'I just passed a cheshire cat and I'm getting fed up with this puzzle.. It's taking forever. But, How long is forever? I don't know, but just add giveit in front of the answer and you can fall in the keyhole.' The Cheshire Cat is from Alice's Adventures in Wonderland. The famous exchange is: 'How long is forever?' / White Rabbit: 'Sometimes, just one second.' So the core answer is 'just one second' = justonesecond, and the riddle literally tells you to glue 'giveit' on the front: giveitjustonesecond.

STEP 4 - Solve Riddle 3 (physics). The text reads: 'The fundamental limit to the precision with which certain pairs of physical properties are know[n].' That is the textbook definition of Heisenberg's uncertainty principle in quantum physics (you can't know both a particle's exact position and momentum at the same time). The answer, lowercase and no spaces: heisenbergsuncertaintyprinciple.

STEP 5 - Understand the casing/spacing rule. Each riddle line ends with the annotation '/(aa,connected enf)'. This is the puzzle's shorthand telling you HOW to write the answers: 'aa' means keep it all lowercase (like the example aa, not Aa or AA), and 'connected'/'enf' means connect the words with NO spaces. That is why every answer is lowercase and run-together.

STEP 6 - Concatenate the three answers. Join them in order, lowercase, no spaces or punctuation between them: jacquefresco + giveitjustonesecond + heisenbergsuncertaintyprinciple = jacquefrescogiveitjustonesecondheisenbergsuncertaintyprinciple.

STEP 7 - Hash the concatenation to get the next password. Feed that combined string into SHA-256. The result is: 250f37726d6862939f723edc4f993fde9d33c6004aab4f2203d9ee489d61ce4c. (You can do this in a terminal with: printf '%s' 'jacquefrescogiveitjustonesecondheisenbergsuncertaintyprinciple' | sha256sum - the printf without a trailing newline matters, because an extra newline would change the hash.)

STEP 8 - Use that hash to decrypt the next blob (phase 3.2). The phase3.txt text even tells you: 'Phase 3.2 is ciphered with aes-256-cbc base64 and a sha256 pw, yet again.' Run: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -a -md sha256 -in phase32.txt -pass pass:250f37726d6862939f723edc4f993fde9d33c6004aab4f2203d9ee489d61ce4c  (the -d means decrypt, -a means the input is base64 text, and -md sha256 tells OpenSSL how to stretch the password - omitting it gives 'bad decrypt').

STEP 9 - Read the reward. The phase 3.2 blob decrypts cleanly to the 'Architect' monologue lifted from The Matrix Reloaded: 'I've been waiting for you. You have many questions, and although the process has altered your consciousness, you remain irrevocably human...'. beginnerExplainers: Hash (SHA-256): a one-way fingerprint machine - same text in always gives the same 64-character code out, but you can't reverse it. Like a meat grinder: easy to make mince from steak, impossible to rebuild the steak.
Encryption / AES-256-CBC: scrambling a message with a password so only someone with the password can unscramble it. Like a locked diary where the password is the key.

Salt: a short, NON-secret random value stored at the front of an encrypted file and mixed with the password so the same password never produces identical-looking output twice. Like adding a pinch of a known spice so two identical soups taste slightly different - it doesn't hide the recipe, it just varies the result.

OpenSSL: a free command-line program that performs the encryption/decryption. The 'U2FsdGVkX1' you see at the start of every blob is just the word 'Salted__' written in base64, the tell-tale sign an OpenSSL password was used.

Base64: a way of writing raw binary data using only ordinary letters, digits, +, and / so it can be pasted as plain text. The encrypted blobs are stored in base64; the '-a' flag tells OpenSSL to expect it.
Concatenation: just gluing strings end-to-end with nothing in between (cat + dog = catdog). Here the three lowercase answers are concatenated before hashing.

Quantum uncertainty (Heisenberg's principle): a law of physics saying you cannot simultaneously know a particle's exact position and its exact momentum - measuring one blurs the other. It's the 'answer' to riddle 3, not something you need to calculate.

finding.summary: Phase 3.2 is the "Architect" stage of the GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle. After unlocking an AES-encrypted blob, the solver finds two intertwined sub-puzzles: (1) a letters blob that is Beaufort-deciphered with the key THEMATRIXHASYOU to reveal a Matrix-style "Architect" monologue, and (2) a long digit string that is run through a VIC (straddling-checkerboard) cipher to reveal the line "IN CASE YOU MANAGE TO CRACK THIS THE PRIVATE KEYS BELONG TO HALF AND BETTER HALF AND THEY ALSO NEED FUNDS TO LIVE." All the specific values claimed (Beaufort key, VIC alphabet FUBCDORA.LETHINGKYMVPS.JQZXW, the numeric string, the decoded text, the AES salt eefc4c5befc1656a) are independently corroborated. The phase itself is SOLVED; it only sets up the still-OPEN endgame (Salphaseion / Cosmic Duality), and the prize address remains funded and unclaimed.
stepByStep: WHAT YOU'RE LOOKING AT: A 'cipher' is a recipe for scrambling a message so only someone who knows the secret can read it. A 'private key' is the secret password that controls a Bitcoin wallet — whoever holds it owns the coins. This puzzle hides clues that, in theory, chain together to reveal such a key. Phase 3.2 is one link in that chain.

HOW YOU ARRIVE HERE: Each phase's answer is turned into a 'fingerprint' (a SHA-256 hash) that becomes the password for the next encrypted file. By Phase 3.2 you have already unlocked earlier doors, so you now hold an AES-256-encrypted file (the blob in ciphertexts/phase32.txt). AES is just a much stronger, modern lock than the old paper ciphers.

UNLOCK THE BLOB: Decrypt phase32.txt with OpenSSL using the chained passphrase. The file begins with the bytes 'Salted__' followed by an 8-byte 'salt'. A salt is a small bit of random data mixed into the password so the same password never produces the same locked file twice — it stops attackers from using pre-made lookup tables. This blob's salt is eefc4c5befc1656a (you can see it yourself: the 9th through 16th bytes of the decoded file).

WHAT'S INSIDE: The decrypted blob contains two things to crack by hand — a block of jumbled LETTERS and a long block of DIGITS. The puzzle text hints at the tools: 'one for one, four for one' (pointing to IBM code page / EBCDIC-1141 for an early decoding step, and to using the digits 1 and 4 in the VIC cipher), and a Matrix reference 'the matrix has you'.

THE BEAUFORT CIPHER (the letters): A Beaufort cipher is a paper-and-pencil cipher. Imagine the 26 letters laid out A-Z. You pick a secret keyword and repeat it under your message. For each letter you do a simple reversible lookup in a square grid. Beaufort's quirk is that encrypting and decrypting are the SAME operation, so you don't need to know which direction you're going. Here the keyword is THEMATRIXHASYOU (from the Matrix quote 'the matrix has you').

RUN IT: Feed the letters block and the key THEMATRIXHASYOU into any Beaufort tool (e.g. dcode.fr's Beaufort solver). Out comes the 'Architect' speech — a long monologue paraphrasing the Architect character from The Matrix Reloaded, beginning 'YOUR LIFE IS THE SUM OF A REMAINDER OF AN UNBALANCED EQUATION...' and ending '...I REALLY HOPE YOURE THE ONE CIAO BELLA O'. It is flavour text plus hints (e.g. 'WHAT A WISEMAN ABOVE HINTED AT IS WORTH HUNDRED FOURTY OF THE INVESTMENT').

THE VIC CIPHER (the digits): The VIC cipher is a famous Cold-War Soviet spy cipher. Its core trick is the 'straddling checkerboard': a small table that turns letters into digits, where the most common letters get a single digit and rarer letters get two digits (the first of which is a 'marker' digit). This lets a short message become a stream of numbers with no spaces. Here the marker/straddling digits are 1 and 4 (that's the 'one for one, four for one' hint).

BUILD THE BOARD: The checkerboard alphabet is FUBCDORA.LETHINGKYMVPS.JQZXW. This odd ordering is itself a riddle answer — derived from the flavour phrase about 'a fubcd-king & oracle-queen, thingky mvps, on a sad board...'. The two '.' marks are the placeholders that sit above the marker columns. You arrange these letters into the checkerboard with rows 1 and 4 as the straddling rows.

DECODE THE NUMBERS: Take the numeric string 15165943121972409169171213758951813141543131412428154191312181219433121171617137149110916631213131281491109166131412199114371612126021664313711154112 and read it through the board: a 1 or 4 means 'look in the straddling row, this digit plus the next one give a letter'; any other digit maps straight to a single letter. The result is the sentence: 'IN CASE YOU MANAGE TO CRACK THIS THE PRIVATE KEYS BELONG TO HALF AND BETTER HALF AND THEY ALSO NEED FUNDS TO LIVE'.

WHY IT MATTERS: That decoded sentence is the big reveal of the phase — it tells you the final prize is NOT one private key sitting in plain sight. It is split between 'HALF' and 'BETTER HALF' (a two-part / shared-key idea), and the creators editorialise that they also 'need funds to live'. This reframes the endgame as a key-assembly problem, not a simple decode.

WHERE THIS LEAVES YOU (solved vs open): Phase 3.2 is fully SOLVED and well-documented. It hands you flavour, the 'half & better half' concept, and the next chained passphrase. What remains OPEN is the endgame: the Salphaseion / 'Cosmic Duality' stage needs two values nicknamed 'yellowblueprimes' (from a block called dbbi) and 'yinyang' (from a block called faed), combined as sha256(yellowblueprimes·matrixsumlist·lastwordsbeforearchichoice·yinyang). Those are NOT solved — do not trust anyone claiming a finished solution unless they show a spendable key.

VERIFY IT YOURSELF: The prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe still holds about 1.256 BTC (125,634,510 satoshis) and has never been emptied by a solver. The prize halves at each Bitcoin halving (5 → 2.5 → 1.25 BTC), which matches the current balance. A funded, unspent address is the ground-truth proof the puzzle is genuinely unsolved.

beginnerExplainers: Cipher: a reversible recipe for scrambling letters so only someone with the secret can read them — like a decoder ring.
Private key: the single secret password that controls a Bitcoin wallet; whoever knows it owns the coins inside.

Hash (SHA-256): a one-way 'fingerprint' machine — same input always gives the same 64-character fingerprint, but you can't run it backwards. Used here to turn each phase's answer into the next phase's password.
Salt: a pinch of random data mixed into a password before encrypting, so the same password never makes the same locked file twice — defeats pre-made cracking tables.
AES-256-CBC: the strong modern 'lock' used for the encrypted blobs; OpenSSL writes it with a 'Salted__' header followed by the salt, which is why we can read the salt straight off the file.
Beaufort cipher: a paper cipher using a keyword and a letter grid, with the unusual property that encrypting and decrypting are the exact same operation.
Straddling checkerboard: a little table that turns letters into digits, giving common letters one digit and rare letters two — the heart of the VIC cipher.
VIC cipher: a famously strong hand cipher used by a real Soviet spy, built on the straddling checkerboard plus number juggling; here only the checkerboard step is needed.
EBCDIC / code page 1141: an old IBM way of representing text as bytes (different from today's ASCII/Unicode); the first sub-step re-reads raw bytes through this table.
Satoshi: the smallest unit of Bitcoin (1 BTC = 100,000,000 satoshis); a balance of 125,634,510 sat means about 1.256 BTC.
discrepancies: The two community walkthroughs disagree on the exact VIC checkerboard notation: puzzlehunt and the local guide use 'FUBCDORA.LETHINGKYMVPS.JQZXW', while Naddiseo writes 'fubcdora/lethingkymvpszjqwx.' (same derivation, different trailing letter order and '/' vs '.'). The puzzlehunt/local form is the community-consensus version, but a beginner reproducing it should be aware the boards differ slightly.

The puzzle is universally branded a '5 BTC' puzzle, but the prize address currently holds only ~1.256 BTC. This is expected, not an error: the creator halves the prize at each Bitcoin halving (5 → 2.5 → 1.25). Newcomers may be confused by '5 BTC' marketing vs the live balance.

GitHub issues titled 'SOLVED' (e.g. #56, #69, and a Dec 2025 claim) assert full solutions, but the prize address is still funded and unspent — so these claims are unverified/refuted by the blockchain. The decoded VIC line ('private keys belong to HALF and BETTER HALF') itself implies the final prize is a split/assembled key, not a plaintext key, which is why confident 'solved' posts have not yielded a spendable key.

The next-stage AES passphrase (what the Phase 3.2 answer chains into) is described in the local guide as part of the chain, but the actual recovery of the endgame values yellowblueprimes/yinyang is NOT solved; sources that gesture at a complete chain past 3.2 should not be taken as proof.

finding.summary: This phase is the bridge between the early "matrix/Matrix-movie" phases and the deep encrypted "SalPhaseIon" branch of the GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle. A side hint lived in the virtual world Decentraland (a clickable box at LAND parcel -41,-17 that plays an audio file). The audio is steganography: split the stereo into left and right channels, invert (phase-flip) one channel and sum them so the common parts cancel and a hidden layer survives, then view it as a spectrogram (a picture of the sound's frequencies), where the words HASHTHETEXT appear. Following that instruction, you take ALL the text written on the very first puzzle image — the label plus the prize Bitcoin address, i.e. GSMGIO5BTCPUZZLECHALLENGE1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe — and run it through SHA-256. The resulting hash 89727c598b9cd1cf8873f27cb7057f050645ddb6a7a157a110239ac0152f6a32 is used as a secret page name: visiting gsmg.io/<that hash> opens the SalPhaseIon / "Cosmic Duality" phase. Every specific value in the published claim is independently confirmed; SalPhaseIon itself is only partially decoded and its endgame (Cosmic Duality, gated by the unsolved dbbi/faed blocks) remains OPEN.
stepByStep: WHAT YOU'RE TRYING TO DO: Find the hidden web address (URL) of the next puzzle stage, called 'SalPhaseIon'. The puzzle never links to it directly; you have to figure out its secret name and type it into your browser yourself.

CONCEPT - a hash (SHA-256): Think of a hash as a 'fingerprint machine'. You feed in any text, and it spits out a fixed 64-character code (made of digits 0-9 and letters a-f). The same input ALWAYS gives the same fingerprint, but you can't run it backwards to recover the input. The puzzle uses these fingerprints as secret door names.

CONCEPT - what Decentraland is: Decentraland is a 3D virtual world you walk around in a web browser, like a video game map. Places in it have map coordinates written as two numbers, e.g. (-41, -17). The puzzle creators bought a plot of virtual land there and hid a clue on it.

STEP 1 - Visit the Decentraland clue. Go to the GSMG plot in Decentraland at coordinates -41,-17. There is a clickable box/object; clicking it plays a sound. Download that sound file (the community calls it audio_source.wav).

CONCEPT - stereo, channels, and phase inversion: A stereo audio file has two tracks playing at once: a LEFT channel and a RIGHT channel (what your two ears hear). 'Inverting' (phase-flipping) a channel turns its sound wave upside down. If you flip one channel and then ADD the two channels together, everything that was IDENTICAL in both channels cancels out to silence - and only the DIFFERENCE between them remains. The creators hid a secret signal in just that difference, so this trick reveals it.

STEP 2 - Process the audio. Open the file in a free audio editor (e.g. Audacity). Split the stereo track into its separate Left and Right channels. Invert (phase-flip) one of the two channels. Mix/sum the two channels back together (and down to a single mono track). The everyday sound cancels, leaving the hidden layer.

CONCEPT - a spectrogram: A spectrogram is a picture of sound. Time runs left-to-right, pitch (frequency) runs bottom-to-top, and brightness shows how loud each pitch is at each moment. Because it's a picture, you can literally 'draw' letters into a sound by placing energy at the right pitches - which is exactly what the creators did.
STEP 3 - Read the spectrogram. Switch the processed audio to spectrogram view. Up in the high-frequency band you will see letters spelling the instruction: HASHTHETEXT (i.e. 'hash the text').

CONCEPT - 'which text?': The hint 'hash the text' points you back to the very first puzzle image at gsmg.io/puzzle. You take ALL the writing on that original image, including the Bitcoin prize address printed on it, with no spaces and no line breaks.

STEP 4 - Build the exact string. The full text is: GSMGIO5BTCPUZZLECHALLENGE1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe (59 characters: the label 'GSMGIO5BTCPUZZLECHALLENGE1' immediately followed by the prize address '1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe'). Do NOT add a trailing newline - that changes the result.

STEP 5 - Hash it with SHA-256. Run that string through SHA-256. (On a Mac/Linux terminal: printf '%s' 'GSMGIO5BTCPUZZLECHALLENGE1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe' | sha256sum . Or paste it into any 'SHA256' tool such as CyberChef - just make sure no newline is appended.) The output is: 89727c598b9cd1cf8873f27cb7057f050645ddb6a7a157a110239ac0152f6a32

STEP 6 - Open the secret page. That 64-character hash IS the secret page name. Put it after the site address: https://gsmg.io/89727c598b9cd1cf8873f27cb7057f050645ddb6a7a157a110239ac0152f6a32 . This opens the SalPhaseIon ('Cosmic Duality') phase. WHY IT WORKS: the creators pre-named the hidden page with the fingerprint of the starting image's text, so only someone who correctly hashes that exact text can discover the address.

WHERE IT STANDS: Reaching SalPhaseIon this way is SOLVED and reproducible. Inside SalPhaseIon, only some sections are decoded (yielding fragments like matrixsumlist, lastwordsbeforearchichoice, yellowblueprimes, yinyang). The final 'Cosmic Duality' decryption - gated by two still-unsolved encoded blocks nicknamed dbbi and faed - is OPEN. Do not trust any 'I solved it / here's the private key' claim: the prize address remains unclaimed.

beginnerExplainers: Hash (SHA-256): a one-way 'fingerprint machine' - any text in, a fixed 64-character code out; same text always gives the same code, and you can't reverse it. Used here to name a hidden web page.
Decentraland: a 3D virtual world in your browser, like a game map, where places have coordinates (e.g. -41,-17); the creators hid a sound clue on their plot.

Stereo channels and phase inversion: a stereo file has separate Left and Right tracks; flipping one upside-down and adding them cancels everything identical and leaves only the hidden difference - like two transparencies that erase the common ink.
Spectrogram: a picture of sound (time across, pitch up, brightness = loudness); because it's an image, words can be 'painted' into audio and read back as letters - here, HASHTHETEXT.

Bitcoin address vs. private key: the address (1GSMG1JC9...) is like a public mailbox anyone can send coins to; the private key is the secret that lets you spend them. The puzzle's goal is to derive that private key; nobody has.
discrepancies: The community site's README says the prize address is 'unspent', but blockchain explorers show 125 transactions and ~7.5 BTC of outflows, with a current balance of ~1.256 BTC. These reconcile if 'unspent' means the PUZZLE is unsolved (no solver has claimed it via the intended mechanism), while the address itself has had operational/donation activity and the prize was deliberately halved at Bitcoin halvings (5 -> 2.5 -> ~1.25 BTC). Worth stating precisely rather than as a flat 'unspent'.
Some web summaries still quote the prize as '2.5 BTC' (post-2020) while on-chain it is now ~1.25 BTC (post-2024 halving). The lower, on-chain figure is the current one.
