finding.summary: SalPhaseIon is the final solved-and-then-open stage of the GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle. After Phase 3.2, a hash of all the text on the opening image opens the SalPhaseIon page, whose body is one long "soup" of letters drawn from a tiny alphabet, split into chunks by the letter z. Four chunks were cracked: two a/b runs decode (as binary) to "matrixsumlist" and "enter", and two a–i/o runs decode (as base-9 → hex → ASCII) to "lastwordsbeforearchichoice" and "thispassword". Two chunks remain UNSOLVED — nicknamed dbbi (91 symbols) and faed (570 symbols) after their first four letters — which the community believes should become "yellowblueprimes" and "yinyang", the missing ingredients of the SHA-256 passphrase that decrypts the final Cosmic Duality AES blob. I independently reproduced all four decoded tokens and read both AES salts straight out of the real ciphertext files: the SalPhaseIon inner blob salt is 3ab585348552415d and the Cosmic Duality blob salt is 2d3f6fe06dc950e6 — both CONFIRMED. dbbi, faed, yellowblueprimes, yinyang and Cosmic Duality are all genuinely OPEN; nothing here is solved beyond the four tokens and the salts.
stepByStep: WHAT YOU'RE LOOKING AT (concepts first). A 'hash' (here SHA-256) is a one-way fingerprint machine: feed it any text and it spits out a fixed 64-character hex code; the same text always gives the same code, but you cannot run it backwards. 'AES-256-CBC' is a digital padlock: it scrambles a file with a password, and only the exact password unscrambles it. OpenSSL (the standard tool the puzzle uses) wraps an encrypted file in a header that literally starts with the ASCII word 'Salted__' followed by 8 random bytes called the 'salt'; the salt is mixed with your password so the same password produces different scrambles each time. A 'base' is just how many digit-symbols a counting system uses (base-10 = our normal 0-9; base-2/binary = only 0 and 1; base-9 = nine symbols). 'a1z26' means the letters a..z stand for the numbers 1..26. Keep these five ideas (hash, AES, salt, base, a1z26) in mind — every step below uses one of them.

STEP 1 — Get onto the SalPhaseIon page (this is the entry, already solved). The community-documented instruction is: take ALL the text printed on the puzzle's opening image (including the Bitcoin address shown on it), feed that whole text into SHA-256, and the resulting 64-character hex string is the secret URL. Confirmed value: gsmg.io/89727c598b9cd1cf8873f27cb7057f050645ddb6a7a157a110239ac0152f6a32 . WHY it works: the creators pre-computed that hash and named the page after it, so only someone who reproduces the exact image-text gets the exact address — a self-checking 'password is the page name' trick.

STEP 2 — Recognise the 'soup'. The SalPhaseIon page body is a long stream of letters using only the symbols a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h,i and o, with the letter z used as a separator. Split the stream wherever a z appears: you get several chunks. WHY: z is not part of the a..i/o working alphabet, so it can only be punctuation — the author is telling you 'each piece between z's is its own little code'. Four of these chunks were cracked; two were not.

STEP 3 — Crack the two a/b chunks (binary). Two chunks contain only the letters a and b (e.g. one starts 'abba...'). Replace a→0 and b→1, then read the 0/1 string as 8-bit binary ASCII (group the bits in eights, turn each group into the byte it represents). WHY: a and b are just a disguised 0 and 1, and 8 bits = one character is the universal ASCII rule. Result, which I re-computed myself: the 104-bit chunk → 'matrixsumlist'; the 40-bit chunk → 'enter'. CONFIRMED.

STEP 4 — Crack the two a–i/o chunks (base-9 to hex). The other two solved chunks use a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h,i and o. Map them by position: a=1, b=2, c=3, d=4, e=5, f=6, g=7, h=8, i=9, and o=0 (so the alphabet is a 'base-9-style' set of nine letters plus a zero). Substitute every letter for its digit to get one big number, then convert that number into hexadecimal, then read each pair of hex digits as one ASCII character. WHY: this is the same a1z26 idea but only 1-9 plus 0, and hex pairs are a standard way to spell ASCII. Result, which I re-computed: the 'agda...' chunk → 'lastwordsbeforearchichoice'; the 'cfob...' chunk → 'thispassword'. CONFIRMED.

STEP 5 — Identify the two UNSOLVED chunks. After the four solved chunks, two long chunks refuse to decode by the same methods. They are nicknamed by their first four letters, exactly like 'agda' and 'cfob' above: one begins d-b-b-i (so 'dbbi', 91 symbols long, 91 = 7×13) and one begins f-a-e-d (so 'faed', 570 symbols long, 570 = 2·3·5·19). IMPORTANT clarification: '91 in base-9' / '570 in base-9' means these chunks are 91 and 570 SYMBOLS long written in the nine-letter alphabet — NOT that they decode to the literal number 91 or 570. These two chunks are the whole open problem.

STEP 6 — Understand what dbbi and faed are SUPPOSED to become. Separate puzzle hints (a reverse-binary 'master' message and the creators' chatter) say the final answer is built from four ingredients in this order: yellowblueprimes · matrixsumlist · lastwordsbeforearchichoice · yinyang. You already have matrixsumlist and lastwordsbeforearchichoice from Step 3-4. The belief is that dbbi hides the value 'yellowblueprimes' and faed hides the value 'yinyang'. THIS IS A HYPOTHESIS, NOT A SOLUTION — no one has actually turned dbbi into 'yellowblueprimes' or faed into 'yinyang'.

STEP 7 — Know the two real AES boxes and their salts. There is a SalPhaseIon inner encrypted blob and the final 'Cosmic Duality' encrypted blob, both real OpenSSL 'Salted__' files. I opened the raw ciphertext files and read bytes 9-16 (the salt) directly: SalPhaseIon inner blob salt = 3ab585348552415d; Cosmic Duality blob salt = 2d3f6fe06dc950e6. CONFIRMED from the artifacts themselves. The salt is fixed and public (it's in the file); it is NOT the secret — the secret is the passphrase.

STEP 8 — The endgame, stated honestly. To open Cosmic Duality you must (a) recover yellowblueprimes from dbbi and yinyang from faed, (b) glue the four ingredients together in the correct order/format, (c) SHA-256 that glued string to make the passphrase, and (d) AES-256-CBC-decrypt the Cosmic blob with it. A correct passphrase is recognised because the AES padding (PKCS7) validates and readable text appears; every wrong guess fails with a padding error. WHY this is still open: steps (a) and (b) are unknown. Be warned the very 'glue' method is disputed — one community write-up (issue #56) argues for XORing seven SHA-256 word-hashes instead of concatenating four words, so even the recipe shape is not settled. DO NOT trust any 'I solved it' claim that hasn't actually moved the prize coins.

STEP 9 — How to verify all of this yourself with free tools. For the tokens: paste each chunk into CyberChef (gchq.github.io/CyberChef) with recipe Substitute('ab','01') then From_Binary for the a/b chunks, or Substitute('abcdefghio','1234567890') then To_Base(16) then From_Hex for the a-i/o chunks. For the salts and AES: run `openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -a -md sha256 -in ciphertexts/cosmic.txt -pass pass:YOURGUESS` — note the required `-md sha256`; on a wrong guess it errors, on the right one it prints plaintext. You can also just open the community compendium's index.html in a browser, which runs the exact same crypto live.

beginnerExplainers: Hash (SHA-256): a one-way fingerprint machine — same text always gives the same 64-character code, but you can't run it backwards to recover the text. Like a meat grinder: easy to make mince, impossible to rebuild the steak.
AES-256-CBC encryption: a digital padlock that scrambles a file with a password; only the exact password unscrambles it. Wrong password = jammed lock (a 'padding error').

Salt (the 'Salted__' header): 8 random bytes stored in the open at the start of an OpenSSL file and stirred into the password so the same password scrambles differently each time. The salt is public (it's in the file) — it's seasoning, not the secret.

Base / base-9: how many digit-symbols a counting system has. We normally use base-10 (0-9). Here nine letters a-i act like digits 1-9 and 'o' acts like 0, so it's a nine-symbol (plus zero) writing of numbers.
a1z26 cipher: letters stand for numbers by position — a=1, b=2, ... z=26. The puzzle uses a cut-down version (a-i = 1-9, o = 0).
Binary / ASCII: computers store every character as 8 on/off bits (a byte); reading a string of 0s and 1s in groups of eight turns it back into letters. Here a and b are just disguised 0 and 1.
Hexadecimal: base-16 (digits 0-9 then a-f); two hex digits make one byte, which is the standard shorthand for spelling out ASCII characters.

Bitcoin address vs balance: an address is like a transparent mailbox anyone can read on the public ledger; 'unspent' means coins sent there have never been moved out. Anyone can check it on a 'block explorer' such as mempool.space.
discrepancies: The local README/compendium says the prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe is 'unspent'. On-chain data (blockchain.info and mempool.space agree) shows 6 spent outputs, 7.50353498 BTC moved out, and a current balance of only ~1.25634510 BTC across 125 transactions. The honest statement is: the address still holds the live, never-swept prize (~1.256 BTC after the creators' scheduled halving withdrawals), but it is NOT literally 'unspent'. Recommend correcting that wording.

Wording trap: 'dbbi (91 in base-9)' and 'faed (570 in base-9)' describe how MANY symbols each block has (91 and 570 symbols written in the nine-letter alphabet), not a decoded numeric value of 91 or 570. A beginner could misread these as the answers. The numbers are lengths/factorisations (91=7x13, 570=2x3x5x19), used only to guess possible grid/strand layouts.

The final Cosmic Duality key recipe is contested. The compendium and the reverse-binary 'master hint' favour sha256(yellowblueprimes·matrixsumlist·lastwordsbeforearchichoice·yinyang) — four words concatenated. Issue #56 instead claims the key is the XOR of SHA-256 hashes of seven tokens (a 'Half and Better Half' split-key). Neither has ever produced a valid decryption, so even the SHAPE of the final step is unsolved — do not present either as fact.

The mapping dbbi->'yellowblueprimes' and faed->'yinyang' is a community hypothesis, NOT a result. The compendium's own dead-end ledger records that the literal string 'yellowblueprimes' is absent from dbbi in every base, and that exhaustive substitution/transposition/polyalphabetic sweeps of both blocks produced only garbage. These targets remain guesses.

title: Cosmic Duality — the endgame recipe and open status

finding.phase: Cosmic Duality — the endgame recipe and open status
finding.summary: Cosmic Duality is the final, still-unsolved stage of the GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle. The 2023-02-23 official "reverse-binary" hint decodes to a sentence that names the four password ingredients (yellowblueprimes, matrixsumlist, lastwordsbeforearchichoice, yinyang) plus a closing taunt that the password is "in front of your eyes but you're not seeing it" — i.e. the hint names the parts but deliberately withholds how to combine them. The exact endgame operation (sha256 of the four tokens, used to AES-decrypt the Cosmic Duality blob into a Bitcoin private key) is a plausible community HYPOTHESIS, not a confirmed working recipe: no authoritative source shows it actually decrypting the blob, and the prize is still unclaimed in 2026. Two of the four tokens (matrixsumlist, lastwordsbeforearchichoice) are genuinely recovered from the SalPhaseIon stage; the other two (yellowblueprimes -> the "dbbi" block, yinyang -> the "faed" block) are only named by the hint and have not been independently decoded.
stepByStep: WHAT THE PUZZLE IS (from zero): In 2017 the GSMG.io team locked 5 real bitcoins (worth a fortune) behind a chain of riddles. Whoever solves the final riddle learns a secret number (a 'private key') that lets them move those coins to themselves. Nobody has done it. Solving each stage gives you the key to the next locked door.

CONCEPT — HASH (SHA-256): A hash is a one-way fingerprint machine. You feed it any text and it spits out a fixed 64-character code. The same text always gives the same code, but you cannot run it backwards to recover the text. The puzzle uses this as a lock: the answer to a riddle is hashed, and the resulting code is the key that opens the next box. Analogy: blending fruit into a smoothie — easy to blend, impossible to un-blend back into whole fruit.

CONCEPT — ENCRYPTION (AES) and PASSPHRASE: Encryption scrambles a message so it looks like random gibberish; only someone with the correct password (passphrase) can unscramble it. GSMG uses a standard tool called OpenSSL with AES-256-CBC. Throughout the puzzle the password handed to OpenSSL is itself the 64-character SHA-256 code from the previous step. Analogy: a locked diary; the passphrase is the only key that turns readable pages back from gibberish.

CONCEPT — BITCOIN PRIVATE KEY and WIF: A Bitcoin address (like 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe) is like a transparent mailbox anyone can see and send to, but only the matching private key can OPEN it to spend. When written in the standard human-readable form called WIF (Wallet Import Format), that key is a ~51-52 character string that starts with 5 (older 'uncompressed' style) or with K or L ('compressed' style). The puzzle's real prize is this WIF string.

HOW WE GOT HERE (the solved path, briefly): Phase 0/1 — a 14x14 grid of coloured squares is read as binary (black/blue=1, yellow/white=0) counter-clockwise in a spiral, spelling the URL gsmg.io/theseedisplanted. Phases 2-3.2 — a chain of movie/cipher riddles (The Matrix 'causality', Mr Robot, a chess problem, Jacque Fresco, Heisenberg, an EBCDIC+Beaufort+VIC cipher block) each produce a passphrase that is hashed and used to AES-decrypt the next page. These phases are SOLVED and reproducible.

ENTERING THE ENDGAME (SalPhaseIon): A creator hint ('Roses are White but often Red...') says go back to the first puzzle image and hash the text on it. Hashing the literal string GSMGIO5BTCPUZZLECHALLENGE1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe gives 89727c598b9cd1cf8873f27cb7057f050645ddb6a7a157a110239ac0152f6a32, and gsmg.io/<that hash> is the SalPhaseIon page — the gateway to the final stage called Cosmic Duality.

DECODING SALPHASEION (partly solved): The SalPhaseIon page is a wall of letters in several encodings, separated by the letter 'z'. The 'abba' runs decode by setting a=0,b=1 and reading the result as binary ASCII, giving 'matrixsumlist' and 'enter'. The 'shabef' marker tells you a=1..z=26 maps letters to numbers (shabef -> sha256). Other runs (using a=1..i=9, o=0, then converting to base-16 hex -> ASCII) give 'lastwordsbeforearchichoice' and 'thispassword'. So 'matrixsumlist' and 'lastwordsbeforearchichoice' are GENUINELY RECOVERED here. An AES blob on this page (and the Cosmic Duality blob) remain locked.

THE 2023-02-23 'REVERSE-BINARY' MASTER HINT: On 23 Feb 2023 the creators posted what looks like a binary string. Decoded (and reading it in the puzzle's whitespace-removed convention) it reads: yellowblueprimes · matrixsumlist · lastwordsbeforearchichoice · yinyang · wewontgiveawaythepassword · itsinfrontofyoureyesbutyourenotseeingit · verylaststepisatruegiveaway · promised. In plain English: 'yellow blue primes, matrix sumlist, last words before archichoice, yinyang — we won't give away the password, it's in front of your eyes but you're not seeing it, very last step is a true giveaway, promised.'

WHY THIS HINT MATTERS — AND WHAT IT WITHHOLDS: The first FOUR words (yellowblueprimes, matrixsumlist, lastwordsbeforearchichoice, yinyang) are understood to be the four ingredients of the final password. The remaining words are a deliberate taunt: the creators are explicitly saying they will NOT hand over the password and that the missing piece is hidden in plain sight. So the hint NAMES the parts but does NOT tell you the exact operation to combine them — that is the open puzzle.

THE CANDIDATE ENDGAME RECIPE (HYPOTHESIS, NOT CONFIRMED): The community's working guess for the final operation is: privkey = AES-decrypt( CosmicDuality_blob , key = sha256('yellowblueprimes' + 'matrixsumlist' + 'lastwordsbeforearchichoice' + 'yinyang') ). In words: glue the four tokens together, take their SHA-256 fingerprint, and use that 64-character code as the OpenSSL passphrase to unscramble the Cosmic Duality box; the unscrambled text should be the Bitcoin WIF private key. IMPORTANT: this exact recipe has never been shown to actually work — every tested guess fails with an 'invalid padding' error, which is what a wrong password looks like.

WHY TWO INGREDIENTS ARE STILL MISSING: matrixsumlist and lastwordsbeforearchichoice are concretely derived from SalPhaseIon. But 'yellowblueprimes' and 'yinyang' are only NAMED by the hint — they correspond to two still-undecoded blocks the community nicknames 'dbbi' (-> should yield yellowblueprimes) and 'faed' (-> should yield yinyang). Until those blocks are decoded into the precise byte strings the puzzle wants, you don't actually know the literal text to feed into the SHA-256, so you cannot build the real key. ('primes'/'yinyang' point at unused number/duality clues from earlier hints.)

HOW TO TRY IT YOURSELF (and confirm it's open): Save the Cosmic Duality ciphertext to a file cosmic.txt. In a terminal run: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -a -md sha256 -in cosmic.txt -pass pass:<your-64-char-sha256>. For the known-good earlier phases this prints readable English (e.g. Phase 2 opens with pass eb3efb51...). For Cosmic Duality every candidate so far returns 'bad decrypt / padding' — proving the correct passphrase has not yet been found. The note '-md sha256' matters because OpenSSL's key-derivation default changed; without it the bytes don't line up.

WHAT 'SUCCESS' WOULD LOOK LIKE: If someone ever finds the right passphrase, the Cosmic Duality blob will decrypt to readable text containing a Bitcoin private key in WIF form — a string starting with 5, K, or L. Loading that key into a wallet would let them spend the prize sitting at the GSMG addresses. As of June 2026 this has not happened: the funds are unmoved and unclaimed.

DO NOT TRUST 'SOLVED' POSTS: Many confident 'I solved it' write-ups exist (e.g. GitHub issue #69 'SOLVED', issue #51's PGP-key theory, issue #84 'it's unsolvable'). None produces a spendable key or actually decrypts Cosmic Duality, and several are AI-generated. The simple test above disproves them in seconds. Treat any claimed solution as false until it shows the blob decrypting to a working WIF and/or the coins moving on-chain.

Two of the four password ingredients (yellowblueprimes, yinyang) are only NAMED by the 2023-02-23 hint and are NOT yet recovered from the actual dbbi/faed blocks (the user's own dead-end ledger notes 'yellowblueprimes' is absent from dbbi in every base). So even the literal text to hash is not fully known — a key caveat for anyone treating the recipe as ready-to-run.

Prize value drift across sources: puzzlehunt says current value 2.5 BTC (one halving); Naddiseo says 1.25 BTC after a second halving (Apr 2024); blockchain balance (~1.256 BTC in 1GSMG + 3.75 unspent in 17ucy) is consistent with the post-2024 1.25/3.75 split. Use the 2024-halving figures.

verdict.phase: Cosmic Duality — the endgame recipe and open status
redFlags: Search-engine summaries for this puzzle confidently state 'the puzzle has been solved' (sourced from GitHub issue #69). This is FALSE/unverified: issue #69 provides no spendable WIF, has no team confirmation, the coins are unmoved, and the content has AI-generated hallmarks. Anyone relying on web search snippets alone would be misled. The finding correctly resists this.

The finding's high-stakes claim (the sha256-of-four-tokens endgame recipe) is the kind of thing that is usually fabricated/over-confident in this space, but here it is explicitly and correctly labeled 'unverified / HYPOTHESIS, not confirmed' — no over-claim to refute.

Naddiseo's MAIN section heading for the hint omits 'reverse' (just 'Official Hint'); only the table-of-contents entry adds 'Reverse binary string'. The 'reverse-binary' descriptor is real but thinly sourced (one TOC line), so it is corroborated but not deeply attested.

title: The crypto method + the false-solutions landscape

finding.phase: The crypto method (OpenSSL Salted__ / AES-256-CBC / EVP_BytesToKey-SHA256) + the false-solutions landscape
finding.summary: The GSMG.io 5 BTC puzzle wraps each of its later stages in OpenSSL's classic "Salted__" container: AES-256-CBC, with the key and IV derived from a passphrase via EVP_BytesToKey (the old, non-PBKDF2 OpenSSL KDF) using SHA-256 as the digest. I independently confirmed the core mechanics: the base64 "U2FsdGVkX1" prefix really is "Salted__"; on modern OpenSSL the SHA-256 digest is the default (so omitting -md gives the same result, while old OpenSSL ≤1.0.2 defaulted to MD5 and would need -md sha256 explicitly); and the Phase-2 passphrase "causality" hashes to the published key eb3efb51... The salphaseion blob and the deepest layer ("Cosmic Duality", with internal sections nicknamed dbbi/faed) remain genuinely UNSOLVED. The prize address 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe still holds ~1.256 BTC (cross-confirmed on blockchain.info and mempool.space), which by itself proves no one has actually solved it. The numerous "SOLVED" posts (GitHub issues #56, #69, etc.) are false/AI-generated: each one admits it produced no spendable key and invents a fictional "Half and Better Half / split-key / dusting-trigger" story.
stepByStep: WHAT THE PRIZE IS. There is a Bitcoin address, 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe, that holds the reward. A Bitcoin address is like a transparent glass safe: anyone can see what is inside, but only the holder of a secret number called the 'private key' can open it and move the coins. The whole puzzle is a long trail that (in theory) ends in that private key. To 'solve' the puzzle means to walk out with a private key that empties this address. Nothing less counts.

HOW WE KNOW IT IS STILL UNSOLVED. I checked the address on two independent block explorers (blockchain.info and mempool.space). Both agree exactly: balance = 125,634,510 satoshis (1 satoshi = 0.00000001 BTC), so about 1.256 BTC, across 125 transactions. If anyone had found the private key, they would have swept the coins and the balance would be zero. It is not zero. Therefore the puzzle is unsolved, full stop. (It started at 5 BTC and the creator halves the prize at each Bitcoin halving, which is why it is now ~1.25 BTC, not 5.)

CONCEPT: WHAT A HASH IS. A 'hash' (here SHA-256) is a one-way blender: you put in any text and you always get out the same 64-character fingerprint, but you can never run the blender backwards to recover the text. The puzzle uses hashes both to make passwords (hash a phrase to get a 32-byte key) and as gatekeepers (a hidden page is found at a URL derived from a hash).

CONCEPT: WHAT 'ENCRYPTION' AND 'AES-256-CBC' ARE. Encryption scrambles a message so it looks like random noise; only someone with the right key can unscramble it. AES-256-CBC is a specific, extremely common scrambling recipe. AES = the algorithm; 256 = the key is 256 bits (32 bytes) long; CBC = a mode that chains blocks together so identical input blocks don't produce identical output. You do not need to understand the math; you only need the right key and the right tool.

CONCEPT: THE 'Salted__' CONTAINER. When you scramble something with the OpenSSL tool the easy way, OpenSSL writes a little envelope. The first 8 bytes are the literal letters 'Salted__', the next 8 bytes are a random 'salt', and the rest is the scrambled message. A salt is just random seasoning mixed into the password so that the same password produces different-looking output every time, which blocks pre-computed cracking. The puzzle's encrypted blobs are base64 text (a way of writing raw bytes using printable letters). You can recognize a Salted__ blob instantly because its base64 always starts with the letters 'U2FsdGVkX1' — I verified this is exactly what base64('Salted__') produces.

CONCEPT: HOW THE PASSWORD BECOMES A KEY (EVP_BytesToKey). AES needs a 32-byte key and a 16-byte IV (initialization vector, a starter value for CBC mode), but you typed a human password. OpenSSL's old converter, called EVP_BytesToKey, turns one into the other like this: take the salt and password, hash them, then keep hashing the previous result joined with the password and salt again, piling up bytes until you have 48 of them. The first 32 bytes become the AES key, the last 16 become the IV. In formula form: D1 = SHA256(password || salt); D2 = SHA256(D1 || password || salt); D3 = SHA256(D2 || password || salt); then key = (D1||D2)[:32], iv = D3[:16]. This puzzle uses SHA-256 as the hash inside that loop.

THE DIGEST GOTCHA (-md sha256). The hash used inside EVP_BytesToKey is chosen by the -md flag. OpenSSL changed its default: versions up to 1.0.2 defaulted to MD5, while 1.1.0 and later (and 3.x) default to SHA-256. I tested this live: a blob made with SHA-256 decrypts correctly whether you pass '-md sha256' or omit it on modern OpenSSL, but using '-md md5' gives 'bad decrypt'. PRACTICAL RULE: always write -md sha256 explicitly so the command works on every OpenSSL version. That is why the published canonical form includes it even though many walkthroughs leave it off (they were on a modern OpenSSL where it is the default).

THE CANONICAL DECRYPT COMMAND. To unscramble one of these blobs you run, in a terminal: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -a -md sha256 -in BLOBFILE -pass pass:THEKEY . Reading it: 'enc' = encryption tool; '-aes-256-cbc' = the recipe; '-d' = decrypt (not encrypt); '-a' = the input is base64 text; '-md sha256' = use SHA-256 in the key derivation; '-in BLOBFILE' = the file holding the blob; '-pass pass:THEKEY' = the password/key. OpenSSL reads the salt out of the 'Salted__' header automatically, runs EVP_BytesToKey, and prints the message if the key was right (or 'bad decrypt' if wrong). Note: modern OpenSSL prints a harmless 'WARNING: deprecated key derivation used' because this old KDF is weaker than today's PBKDF2 — that warning is expected here and does not mean failure.

WORKED EXAMPLE OF A KEY (Phase 2). The early phases are solved, and you can see the pattern clearly. The Phase-2 password is the word 'causality'. The puzzle does not feed the word directly to AES; it feeds the SHA-256 hash of the word as the -pass value. I computed SHA256('causality') myself and got eb3efb5151e6255994711fe8f2264427ceeebf88109e1d7fad5b0a8b6d07e5bf, which exactly matches the published Phase-2 key. So the real command is: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -a -md sha256 -in phase2.txt -pass pass:eb3efb5151e6255994711fe8f2264427ceeebf88109e1d7fad5b0a8b6d07e5bf .

THE MULTI-PART PASSWORD TRICK (Phase 3). Later phases concatenate several discovered tokens into one long string and then hash that. Phase 3's password string glues together causality + Safenet + Luna + HSM + 11110 + a long hex chunk + the chess position 'B5KR/1r5B/2R5/2b1p1p1/2P1k1P1/1p2P2p/1P2P2P/3N1N2 b - - 0 1', and the SHA-256 of that whole string (1a57c572caf3cf722e41f5f9cf99ffacff06728a43032dd44c481c77d2ec30d5) is the AES key. The lesson: the 'key' is always assembled from clues earlier in the trail, then hashed.

REACHING THE SALPHASEION STAGE. A hidden branch is opened by hashing the puzzle's intro text: SHA256('GSMGIO5BTCPUZZLECHALLENGE1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe'). I computed this and got 89727c598b9cd1cf8873f27cb7057f050645ddb6a7a157a110239ac0152f6a32, matching both community walkthroughs. That hash leads to the 'salphaseion' page, which contains another Salted__/AES-256-CBC blob (base64 starting U2FsdGVkX1+...). Inside it, solvers have decoded fragments — words like 'matrixsumlist', 'enter', 'lastwordsbeforearchichoice', and 'thispassword' — by reading sub-sections encoded in odd bases. These fragments are real partial progress.

WHERE IT GOES DARK (Cosmic Duality / dbbi / faed — OPEN). Past those fragments lies the 'Cosmic Duality' layer (its internal sections are nicknamed dbbi and faed in community notes). This is genuinely UNSOLVED. No published passphrase reliably decrypts it into a Bitcoin private key. One careful researcher even reconstructed a corrupted PGP/OpenSSL structure (recovering a salt 2d3f6fe06dc950e6 and a candidate prime) but still could not finish — they stopped at 'we have a footing to try decrypting next', with no key recovered. Do NOT trust any confident-sounding final passphrase for this layer; none has been verified.

HOW TO TELL A REAL SOLVE FROM A FAKE ONE. There is exactly one proof of a solve: a private key that, when imported into a wallet, controls 1GSMG1JC9wtdSwfwApgj2xcmJPAwx7prBe and can move its ~1.256 BTC — and the on-chain balance would then drop to zero. Everything else is noise. Red flags of a FALSE solution, all present in the famous 'SOLVED' posts: (a) it ends in a 'philosophical message' instead of a key; (b) it invents a 'split key' / 'Half and Better Half' / 'dusting trigger' / 'two new addresses you must fund' mechanism (these do not exist — Bitcoin reward puzzles end in a normal single private key); (c) it claims to 'bypass PBKDF2' with an 'XOR master key' like 818af53d...; (d) the prose reads like an AI report with checkmark bullet lists; (e) crucially, the author never shows a transaction spending the prize.

THE FALSE-SOLUTION LANDSCAPE (concrete examples). GitHub issue #69 ('SOLVED: ... Private Key Reconstruction') and issue #56 ('The final resolution of the stage') both LOOK like solutions but each openly admits the opposite: #56 states 'both balances = 0. Puzzle remains inactive unless funding occurs', and #69 concedes 'THE PRIVATE KEYS BELONG TO HALF AND BETTER HALF AND THEY ALSO NEED FUNDS TO LIVE' — i.e., no spendable key was produced. Issue #84 ('why I believe it's unsolvable') is the honest counterpart: a real solver who decrypted everything reachable, hit the same meta-message 'YOUR LIFE IS THE SUM OF A REMAINDER OF AN UNBALANCED EQUATION...', and concluded the deepest layer may be a dead end or psychological trap. The takeaway: treat any 'I solved it' that does not show an emptied prize address as unverified at best.

WHAT YOU CAN SAFELY DO YOURSELF. Reproduce the verified parts to build trust in the method: (1) install OpenSSL; (2) confirm 'echo -n Salted__ | base64' gives 'U2FsdGVkX18='; (3) confirm SHA256('causality') equals the Phase-2 key; (4) make your own blob with 'echo secret | openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -a -md sha256 -pass pass:test' and decrypt it back with the -d form to feel how EVP_BytesToKey and the Salted__ header work. Once the early phases reproduce exactly, you understand the entire crypto method. The only thing left unsolved is finding the correct passphrase(s) for the Cosmic Duality layer — and that, as of this research, nobody has done.

facts.claim: The puzzle's encrypted blobs use OpenSSL's 'Salted__' container format (base64 begins 'U2FsdGVkX1...').
facts.value: U2FsdGVkX1 == base64 prefix of 'Salted__'; salphaseion blob begins U2FsdGVkX1+...
facts.note: I independently ran base64('Salted__') = 'U2FsdGVkX18=', and both walkthroughs show the salphaseion blob starting with U2FsdGVkX1+.

facts.claim: The cipher is AES-256-CBC.
facts.note: Stated consistently across the puzzlehunt README, Naddiseo README, DeepWiki, and multiple issues; consistent with the 'Salted__' format.

facts.claim: Key and IV are derived from the passphrase via EVP_BytesToKey (the legacy, non-PBKDF2 OpenSSL KDF), iterating D=HASH(D||password||salt) to 48 bytes -> 32-byte key + 16-byte IV.
facts.note: This is the documented OpenSSL behavior for `openssl enc` without -pbkdf2/-iter; I confirmed the live tool emits 'WARNING: deprecated key derivation used', which is exactly the EVP_BytesToKey path. The community 'bypass PBKDF2' framing is misleading: these blobs were never PBKDF2 to begin with.

facts.claim: The digest inside the KDF is SHA-256, requiring -md sha256.
facts.value: -md sha256 (default on OpenSSL >=1.1.0 / 3.x; MD5 was default on <=1.0.2)
facts.note: Nuance: I tested live on OpenSSL 3.5.6 — a SHA-256 blob decrypts correctly with OR without -md sha256 (SHA-256 is the modern default), but fails with -md md5. So -md sha256 is REQUIRED on old OpenSSL and merely redundant on new OpenSSL. Many walkthroughs omit it because they ran modern OpenSSL. The published 'must use -md sha256' claim is correct as a portable rule.

facts.claim: Canonical verification command: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -a -md sha256 -in <blob> -pass pass:<key>
facts.note: This exact form (with per-phase -pass values) appears in the puzzlehunt README walkthrough for the solved phases; I verified the flags behave as described on a live OpenSSL.

beginnerExplainers: Bitcoin address vs private key: the address is a public glass safe you can see into; the private key is the only thing that can open it and move the coins. Solving the puzzle = obtaining that key.
Satoshi: the smallest Bitcoin unit, 1 satoshi = 0.00000001 BTC. The address holds 125,634,510 satoshis ~= 1.256 BTC.
Hash (SHA-256): a one-way fingerprint blender — same input always gives the same 64-hex-character output, and you can never run it backwards.
Encryption / AES-256-CBC: a reversible scrambler; with the right key you unscramble, without it you get noise. AES-256-CBC is one specific, very common recipe.
Salt: random seasoning mixed with a password before key derivation, so the same password yields different ciphertext each time and can't be pre-cracked.
OpenSSL 'Salted__' header: a 16-byte envelope (the word 'Salted__' + 8 random salt bytes) prepended to the ciphertext; in base64 it always shows up as the leading letters 'U2FsdGVkX1'.

EVP_BytesToKey: OpenSSL's old way of turning a human password + salt into the 32-byte key and 16-byte IV by repeatedly hashing — weaker than today's PBKDF2, hence the 'deprecated key derivation' warning.
IV (initialization vector): a 16-byte starter value CBC mode needs so the first block doesn't encrypt predictably; derived alongside the key.
base64: a way to write raw binary bytes using only printable letters/digits so they survive being pasted as text.

-md flag and the MD5/SHA-256 default trap: the digest used inside EVP_BytesToKey. Old OpenSSL (<=1.0.2) defaulted to MD5; new OpenSSL (1.1.0+/3.x) defaults to SHA-256 — so always write -md sha256 to be safe everywhere.
PBKDF2: the modern, deliberately-slow password-to-key function; note the GSMG blobs do NOT use it (they use the older EVP_BytesToKey), so claims about 'bypassing PBKDF2' are a misunderstanding.
Split-key / 'Half and Better Half' / dusting-trigger: a fictional mechanism invented by false solutions; real Bitcoin reward puzzles simply end in one normal private key that spends the prize.
discrepancies: Published claim says the canonical command 'requires -md sha256'. Nuance: on modern OpenSSL (1.1.0+/3.x) SHA-256 is already the default, so omitting -md still works; -md sha256 is only strictly required on old OpenSSL (<=1.0.2) where the default was MD5. The claim is correct as a portable best-practice but slightly overstated as an absolute requirement. Verified live on OpenSSL 3.5.6.

Community 'SOLVED' posts describe 'bypassing PBKDF2 key derivation'. This is inaccurate: the GSMG blobs were encrypted with the legacy EVP_BytesToKey KDF, not PBKDF2, so there is no PBKDF2 to bypass. This framing is a tell of an AI-generated/false write-up.

One automated page-summary attributed the Phase-2 key eb3efb51... to the passphrase 'theflowerblossomsthroughwhatseemstobeaconcretesurface'. That is wrong: I computed SHA256 of that phrase = 5ac40783... The Phase-2 password whose SHA-256 equals eb3efb51... is 'causality'. The phrase 'theflowerblossoms...' is a Phase-1/form clue, not the AES key.

The 'XOR master key 818af53daa3028449f125a2e4f47259ddf9b9d86e59ce6c4993a67ffd76bb402' (issue #69) and the seven-token XOR recipe are unverified and almost certainly fabricated — they do not yield a key that spends the prize address, and the address remains funded.

Prize amount is reported variously as 2.5 BTC (post-2020 halving) in older write-ups vs ~1.25 BTC now. The live on-chain balance (~1.256 BTC) reflects the second halving; older docs quoting 2.5 BTC are simply out of date, not wrong for their time.

verdict.phase: The crypto method (OpenSSL Salted__ / AES-256-CBC / EVP_BytesToKey-SHA256) + the false-solutions landscape

checks.fact: base64('Salted__') == 'U2FsdGVkX18=', so OpenSSL Salted__ blobs start with 'U2FsdGVkX1' in base64.
checks.reasoning: I ran printf '%s' 'Salted__' | base64 locally and got exactly U2FsdGVkX18=. This is a deterministic encoding fact, independently reproducible.

checks.fact: Cipher is AES-256-CBC with key/IV derived via EVP_BytesToKey (legacy non-PBKDF2 KDF), D1=H(pw||salt), Dn=H(Dn-1||pw||salt), key=first32, iv=next16.
checks.reasoning: This is documented OpenSSL behavior for `openssl enc` without -pbkdf2. Live test on OpenSSL 3.5.6 emitted the expected deprecated-KDF behavior and decrypted correctly, consistent with EVP_BytesToKey. Corroborated by OpenSSL docs and both community READMEs.

checks.fact: The digest inside the KDF is SHA-256; -md sha256 is default on OpenSSL >=1.1.0 but MD5 was default on <=1.0.2, so -md sha256 is a portable requirement.
checks.reasoning: Live test on OpenSSL 3.5.6: a SHA-256-made blob decrypted both with -md sha256 and without it (modern default), and FAILED with -md md5 ('bad decrypt').
